coredata.dc:title: Recent advances in nanoscale materials for antibody-based cancer theranostics
coredata.dc:creator: Kukkar, Deepak
Kukkar, Preeti
Kumar, Vanish
Hong, Jongki
Kim, Ki-Hyun
Deep, Akash
coredata.dc:description: The quest for advanced management tools or options of various cancers has been on the rise to efficiently reduce their risks of mortality without the demerits of conventional treatments (e.g., undesirable side effects of the medications on non-target tissues, non-targeted distribution, slow clearance of the administered drugs, and the development of drug resistance over the duration of therapy). In this context, nanomaterials-antibody conjugates can offer numerous advantages in the development of cancer theranostics over conventional delivery systems (e.g., highly specific and enhanced biodistribution of the drug in targeted tissues, prolonged systemic circulation, low toxicity, and minimally invasive molecular imaging). This review comprehensively discusses and evaluates recent advances in the application of nanomaterial–antibody bioconjugates for cancer theranostics for the further advancement in the control of diverse cancerous diseases. Further, discussion is expanded to cover the various challenges and limitations associated with the design and development of nanomaterial-antibody conjugates applicable towards better management of cancer.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The quest for advanced management tools or options of various cancers has been on the rise to efficiently reduce their risks of mortality without the demerits of conventional treatments (e.g., undesirable side effects of the medications on non-target tissues, non-targeted distribution, slow clearance of the administered drugs, and the development of drug resistance over the duration of therapy). In this context, nanomaterials-antibody conjugates can offer numerous advantages in the development of cancer theranostics over conventional delivery systems (e.g., highly specific and enhanced biodistribution of the drug in targeted tissues, prolonged systemic circulation, low toxicity, and minimally invasive molecular imaging). This review comprehensively discusses and evaluates recent advances in the application of nanomaterial–antibody bioconjugates for cancer theranostics for the further advancement in the control of diverse cancerous diseases. Further, discussion is expanded to cover the various challenges and limitations associated with the design and development of nanomaterial-antibody conjugates applicable towards better management of cancer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cancer as a high-mortality disease: current status and challenges in treatment
ce:section.ce:para: In the past two decades or so, there has been a continuous decline in the number of medical products approved by global regulatory agencies (e.g., U.S. Food and Drug Administration (US-FDA) and National Institutes of Health) (Lim et al., 2015; Pene et al., 2009). As such, these agencies have urged the scientific community to develop novel approaches and products of medical significance through intensive and collaborative research efforts. Such efforts are required to combat the escalating mortality rate of cancer and similar diseases.

The increasing number of cancer cases across the globe has emerged as one of the most challenging research frontiers for scientists. Cancer is generally understood to be an autosomal genetic disorder in which progressive mutations lead to uncontrolled cell division and proliferation. Because of the molecular complexity of the interaction between malignant tissue and its surrounding environment, the proliferation of cancerous cells can be promoted via many routes (e.g., limitless DNA duplication, absence of apoptotic events, development of insensitivity to aging signals, evasion of the immune system, continuous growth signals, perpetual angiogenesis, metastasis, deregulation of cellular energetics, and chronic inflammation). The exceptional growth of cancer tissues stems from rapid angiogenesis and leaky and defective vasculature. This phenomenon, called the enhanced permeability and retention (EPR) effect, can facilitate diagnostic and treatment approaches, at least to some extent. The EPR effect is extensively used by researchers developing targeted delivery of drug-loaded anticancer carriers such as polymer microcapsules, microspheres, and liposomes. However, the EPR effect is generally not observed in the central parts of metastatic tumors (Fang et al., 2011). Even if it occurs, it allows only a small fraction of the administered drug carriers to reach their targets (Wilhelm et al., 2016). The localization of drug carriers and anticancer drugs in tumors is also limited by various tumor-specific physiological parameters, such as the presence of phagocytic cells, extracellular matrix, low pH, low oxygen concentration, and high interstitial fluid pressure. Such limitations necessitate the development of new targeted delivery options for therapeutic carriers.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The significance of theranostics in cancer management
ce:section.ce:para: Given the complications associated with cancer treatment, the clinical diagnosis of cancer becomes extremely important to increase the overall survival rate of patients via early and precise diagnosis. The clinical diagnosis of cancer revolves around six fundamental techniques: (1) computed tomography (CT), (2) magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), (3) positron emission tomography (PET), (4) single-photon emission computed tomography (SPECT), (5) x-ray imaging, and (6) ultrasound imaging. Because the human body contains 1014 cells, each of which can turn malignant, the detection probability for malignancy can theoretically be as low as 10−14 (Frangioni 2008). Each imaging technique has its own strengths and shortcomings (Lim et al., 2015), and many specific imaging probes have been developed for scanning tumor tissues. The imaging of tumor tissues has been improved substantially by the development of hybrid imaging techniques (such as PET-MRI, fluorescence molecular tomography (FMT)-CT, and FMT-MRI) (Ren et al., 2016; Zhang et al., 2014b). Such hybrid and multimodal imaging methods equipped with a single multimodal imaging nanoprobe have great potential as diagnostic techniques for cancer recognition (Tempany et al., 2015; Yang et al., 2018; Zhao et al., 2018a). Multimodal imaging should provide critical insights into the biochemical events associated with the development of cancer. The superiority of multimodal imaging nanoprobes over conventional imaging tools has been reported in numerous studies (Chen et al., 2016b; Shahbazi-Gahrouei et al., 2019). The advantages of multimodal imaging nanoprobes can be magnified by the integration of therapeutic moieties (e.g., genes or drugs), which has led to the development of nanotheranostics (Lim et al., 2015).

The shortcomings of conventional therapeutic and diagnostic methods warrant the development of novel molecular-scale methods that can efficiently diagnose and eliminate malignant tumors while increasing patient compliance (also called medication adherence). Therefore, many novel diagnostic and treatment methods have been added to the conventional techniques over the years. Some commonly used methods for the molecular detection of cancerous mutation, in addition to the imaging techniques described above, include gene sequencing (Serratì et al., 2016), immunohistochemistry (Swanson 2015), tumor biopsy (Fass 2008), and fluorescence in situ hybridization (Jehan et al., 2012). These new methods target unique mutation patterns or molecular markers that are characteristic of cancerous cells. Some of the common cancer markers are prostate cancer antigen (PSA), Human epidermal growth factor receptor-2 (HER2), EGFR (epidermal growth factor receptor), Cancer antigen-125 (CA125), and BCR-ABL (B-cell receptor-Abelson murine leukemia). These biomarkers play critical role in diagnosis, predictive prognosis, and therapeutics of cancer. A summarized list of commonly used markers for clinical diagnosis of various cancer types is provided in Table 1S (Supporting information (SI)). For detailed discussion on various cancer biomarkers, readers may refer to some review articles (e.g., Alford et al. (2017), Villalobos and Wistuba (2017), Kohaar et al. (2019), and Gupta et al. (2020)).

Cancer cells are characterized by unrestricted cell division along with malignant characteristics. The natural pillars of cancer treatment comprise surgical removal, radiotherapy, and chemotherapy (Huang et al., 2017a; Lim et al., 2015). Surgical removal of tumors is the primary approach for the treatment of solid tumors (Huang et al., 2017a). However, the metastatic nature of cancer cells provides a stringent challenge for surgery alone as a successful therapeutic modality. The second major approach for cancer eradication comprises radiotherapy, commonly employed as a local treatment procedure to reduce the size of a tumor based on the application of high energy X-rays (Cao et al., 2019). Radiotherapy is generally prescribed for the treatment of ~50% of new cancer cases (Hwang et al., 2018). It is primarily performed in combination with surgical treatment and/or between sequential chemotherapy treatment regimens. Some state-of-the-art radiotherapy techniques for cancer treatment include: volumetric modulated arc therapy, intensity-modulated radiotherapy, stereotactic body radiation therapy, and stereotactic radiosurgery (Hwang et al., 2018). These methods have helped in increasing the efficacy of radiotherapeutic cancer treatment with reduced toxic effects, which would otherwise be observed with conventional radiotherapy-based techniques (Citrin 2017). However, the majority of the cancer patients did not have sufficient radiotherapeutic treatments owing to shortcomings such as supply chain shortage of trained human resource and infrastructure (e.g., treatment equipments) (Grover et al., 2014). In addition, the extensive technological developments (both software and hardware) have caused the radiation therapy to become increasingly dependent on human–machine interactions such as artificial intelligence (Huynh et al., 2020). On the other hand, chemotherapeutic drugs inhibit tumor proliferation by interfering with the: (a) DNA replication process and (b) mitotic division of cancer cells (Schirrmacher 2019). A detailed discussion on chemotherapeutic drugs is provided in section 3.2.1.1. In spite of numerous chemotherapeutic drugs in clinical cancer treatment, their efficacy has always been under radar owing to their shortcomings such as non-selective action, non-targeted delivery and toxicity to normal tissues, and a relatively narrow therapeutic window.

In spite of the various therapeutic modalities developed in the last 40 years, high resistance rates, repetitive mutations, and the risk of recurrence make cancer eradication an elusive ambition across the globe. In light of these complexities associated with cancer treatment, more advanced approaches that can facilitate the integration of imaging moieties with therapeutic molecules within a single platform, so-called theranostics, are needed. In this regard, theranostics can enhance the suppression and elimination of cancer by simultaneously detecting and treating diseased tissues. The treatment of cancer can become more efficient if diagnostic procedures themselves can slow cancerous growth.

The emergence of theranostics reflects the vigorous demand for effective and target-oriented treatment for life-threatening disorders. The major aims of theranostics include increased patient compliance, enhanced therapeutic efficacy, and minimizing the need for high-cost drugs. Important considerations include point-of-care treatment, personalized medicine, and pharmacokinetics (Lim et al., 2015). Moreover, theranostic researchers aim to develop an advanced understanding of both the best way to select a proper drug and its likely therapeutic outcomes at the molecular level. The emergence of theranostics thus creates a unique opportunity to develop novel drugs with increased therapeutic efficacy, as desired by various stakeholders, including regulatory agencies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The potential of nanomaterial–antibody bioconjugates in cancer theranostics
ce:section.ce:para: The unique physical and chemical characteristics of nanomaterials (NMs) improve their suitability to bind with biomolecules, which can enable the development of highly potent theranostic solutions for cancer (Huang et al., 2017b). NMs can be easily conjugated with biological moieties (e.g., enzymes, proteins, receptors, and antibodies (Abs)) for specific diagnostic and therapeutic applications. Appropriately bio-conjugated NMs can be applied as nanomedicines, which essentially comprise three key components (Caruso et al., 2012): (a) organic/inorganic NMs, (b) attached/loaded drug molecules for therapeutic action with or without an imaging contrast agent, and (c) a biological macromolecule that acts as a ligand to impart targeting characteristics. The assembly of these components into a nanomedicine confers the ability to target, diagnose, monitor, and treat various medical conditions.

Numerous innovative applications of nanomedicine in cancer theranostics have already been described. Some notable uses of NMs in theranostics include: as a contrast agent in MRI, photodynamic therapy, photothermal therapy (PTT), and photoacoustic imaging. Among biological molecules, Abs are one of the most potent targeting agents for theranostic cancer treatment. Abs contain unique amino acid sequences that give them binding affinity for specific antigenic epitopes. Ab-based immunotherapy can induce remission in patients who would otherwise suffer from advanced malignancies.

Numerous reports have been published on the therapeutic and theranostic applications of NMs such as liposomes (Lim et al., 2015), porous NMs (Baeza et al., 2017; Horcajada et al., 2009), magnetic nanoparticles (MNPs) (Jun et al., 2008), gold nanoparticles (GNPs) (Jazayeri et al., 2016), silver nanoparticles (Wei et al., 2015), quantum dots (QDs) (Lim et al., 2015), silica nanoparticles (Sun et al., 2017), carbon-based NMs (Saleem et al., 2018), and upconversion nanoparticles (UCNPs) (Wu et al., 2014b). Likewise, the tumor-targeting characteristics of Abs and Ab–drug conjugates are well recognized (Lambert and Berkenblit 2018). Ab-based tumor targeting with the aid of biodegradable NMs has already been reviewed (Shargh et al., 2016). However, to the best of our knowledge, no review has specifically considered the theranostic applications of Ab-conjugated NMs. To fill that research gap, this review describes theranostic research on NMs conjugated with various Abs, with an emphasis on progress made since 2010. We first describe various signal transduction mechanisms involving Ab-based cancer therapy, followed by different conjugation approaches used to combine Abs and NMs. Subsequently, we describe the structural features of various NM–Ab conjugates in terms of nanotheranostics for cancer management. We then provide an overall perspective on their application in cancer-based theranostics. Afterwards, the limitations and future perspectives of such nanoconjugate-based applications are described. Finally, we draw conclusions to help establish an effective road map for the development of future Ab-based nanotheranostics for cancer management.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Abs: signal transduction mechanisms for cancer theranostics and bioconjugation strategies for assembly on nanomaterial surfaces
Signal transduction mechanisms involving ab-based cancer theranostics
ce:section.ce:para: Ab-based theranostics harness specific interactions between anti-tumor Abs and their cell surface receptors. Thus, they constitute an active mode of targeting whereby theranostic nanocarriers are actively modified to facilitate the attachment of tumor-specific Abs for targeted delivery (Shargh et al., 2016). These Abs recognize and bind to receptors overexpressed on the surfaces of cancer cells. Over the years, conjugates of monoclonal antibodies (mAbs) and therapeutic drug molecules have been investigated extensively for their potential to offer selective and effective delivery.

The design of Ab–drug conjugates (ADCs) depends on various parameters, including the Ab type, conjugation chemistry, therapeutic moiety, and cell surface receptors expressed on the cancer cells. Many different types of Ab-based therapeutic medicines (e.g., gemtuzumab ozogamicin, brentuximab vedotin, and ado-trastuzumab emtansine) have been approved for clinical trials (Lambert and Berkenblit 2018). However, in most cases, ADCs suffer from inactivation of the therapeutic drug, probably due to factors such as non-specific interactions, low drug payload, inefficient conjugation methods, and low stability in the complex in vivo environment (Nejadmoghaddam et al., 2019). These limitations of ADCs have led to the development of improved methods for attaching or encapsulating the drug molecules within a nanocarrier (e.g., through the surface conjugation of a nanocarrier and a tumor-specific Ab). Such theranostic systems offer two distinct advantages over conventional ADCs: (a) higher drug payload (a 70-nm NP can carry nearly 2000 molecules of small interfering RNA, whereas the carrying capacity of an Ab molecule is about 10 (Davis et al., 2008)); and (b) reduced activity of the multi-drug resistance (MDR) protein pumps expressed on cancer cells (e.g., acute myeloid leukemia (AML) cells) that would otherwise cause the efflux of drug molecules (Chalouni et al., 2018; Da Silva et al., 2017). An ADC construct called SGN-33A, composed of a humanized anti-CD33 (CD = cluster of differentiation) mAb conjugated with a drug called pyrrolobenzodiazepine dimers (Kung Sutherland et al., 2013), was used to monitor the response of MDR protein pumps. SGN-33A increased intracellular delivery, reduced the inhibitory concentration, and improved anti-tumor activity in AML cell lines (Kung Sutherland et al., 2013). Moreover, the potential anti-tumor effects of Ab-based theranostic systems can also be checked before the use of actual therapeutic assays. Thus, Ab-based theranostics could be used to develop personalized cancer therapeutics. In light of the unique advantages of NM–Ab conjugates for cancer theranostics, the following subsections briefly summarize their various signal transduction pathways.

The effects and mechanisms of Ab-mediated cancer targeting rely on a complex environment of immunomodulatory molecules and immune cells. As such, these mechanisms can be divided into three distinct classes by the types of participating molecules. A detailed description of those mechanisms is reported elsewhere (Hendriks et al., 2017). Here, we summarize the characteristic features and critical role of these mechanisms in targeted therapies. Antibody-based various signaling mechanisms are also exemplified in Table 2S.

As the name indicates, the Ab-dependent cell-mediated cytotoxicity (ADCC) mechanism is governed by effector cells (e.g., natural killer cells, eosinophils, macrophages, or neutrophils of the humoral immune system) that lead to the lysis of target cancer cells expressing specific membrane-bound surface antigens (Zahavi et al., 2018). The ADCC pathway is triggered by binding the Fc region of the opsonized Ab (e.g., IgG) to the activating receptors (FcγRI, FcγRIIa, FcγRIIc, FcγRIIIa, and FcγRIIIb) on the effector cells (Rajasekaran et al., 2015). This binding induces the target cell to release cytokines (e.g., IFN-γ) and cytotoxic proteins (e.g., perforin and granzyme) that subsequently lyse the target cells (Hendriks et al., 2017). The binding profile between individual IgG subclasses and the FcγRs is highly specific and affects the downstream signal transduction pathways. This is the seminal interaction in the blueprint for therapeutic mAbs. The anticancer effects of various mAbs such as trastuzumab, rituximab, and cetuximab were reflected predominantly through ADCC mechanism (Table 2S).

Ab-dependent cellular phagocytosis (ADCP) is an alternate pathway for removing target cancer cells using therapeutic mAbs. Briefly, it involves binding the Fc region of an opsonized mAb to FcR-bearing effector cells (e.g., monocytes, macrophages, and neutrophils), which triggers the phagocytic removal of the target cells by the effector cells. Although macrophages act as effector cells in both ADCC and ADCP, numerous experimental findings have established that ADCP is the preferred mode of mAb-mediated cancer-cell targeting. The ADCP activity of an afucosylated, humanized, anti B7–H3 mAb (DS-5573a) was tested in both in vitro and in vivo conditions (Nagase-Zembutsu et al., 2016). These Abs exhibited macrophage-dependent ADCP activity against three B7–H3-expressing cancer cell lines (NCI–H322, MDA-MB-231, and COLO205). Those observations confirmed that ADCP is a better signal transduction mechanism than ADCC for combating cancer cells in the presence of macrophages. Cancer targeting Abs such as Elotuzumab were seen to exert its effect on cancer cells through ADCP mechanism (Table 2S).

Complement-dependent cytotoxicity (CDC) is an effector mechanism of IgG and IgM Abs. These opsonized Abs bind to a target cell in the presence of the C1q protein in the classical complement cascade, which subsequently triggers the formation of a membrane attacking complex (MAC) (Rogers et al., 2014). The MAC leads to the lysis of the target cells. Although the clinical contribution of CDC is unclear, the occurrence and therapeutic relevance of this mechanism have been demonstrated in several case studies (Peschke et al., 2017; Wang et al., 2020a; Zhang et al., 2019b). Various engineered Abs such as LpMab-21 and EMab-17 are also found to produce CDC of cancer cells (Table 2S).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Bioconjugation strategies for the assembly of NMs over Abs
ce:section.ce:para: Bioconjugation between NMs and Abs can greatly enhance the efficacy of the Abs in various cytotoxic pathways, as described in the preceding sections. Fine tuning the morphological and structural characteristics of NMs can facilitate their controlled and robust conjugation with Abs for cancer theranostics. Engineered bioconjugates have distinctive features such as a high drug payload, multivalent binding of ligands (drugs or Abs), and the potential to bypass the MDR protein pumps present on cancer cells (Davis et al., 2008). A detailed discussion about various bioconjugation approaches is presented in supporting information (SI).

The structural organization of theranostic Ab–NM conjugates is vital to their multifunctional capabilities. A cross-sectional view of a typical nanocomposite for cancer theranostics is shown in Fig. 1 . According to that schematic and many previous reports (Chen et al., 2016a; Fan et al., 2017; Minelli et al., 2010; Zhao et al., 2018b), a multifunctional theranostic nanocomposite has three major components: (a) a drug carrier, (b) the theranostic payload, and (c) a surface-active ligand for targeting of the anticancer drug. The carrier structures should be characterized by appreciable biocompatibility and biodegradability under physiological conditions. In addition, the carrier must have a high surface-area-to-volume ratio to deliver sufficient amounts of the bioactive payload to the desired cancerous tissues. To date, numerous carrier types have been designed using organic and inorganic materials. The theranostic payloads include therapeutically active species (e.g., anticancer drugs, tumor suppressor genes, anticancer proteins, small interfering RNA, chemicals that generate reactive oxygen species (ROS), and stimulus-responsive NMs) and imaging agents (e.g., fluorescent NMs (QDs, semiconductor metal oxide NPs, and UCNPs), and noble metal NMs). The third component of theranostic Ab–NM conjugates is surface-active agents or ligands to: (i) improve the systemic circulation period (e.g., PEG and its derivatives), (ii) cross physiological and anatomical barriers, and (iii) create cancer tissue–specific affinity (e.g., Abs).

Carrier NMs should be able to hold their bioactive payloads using physical encapsulation or chemical conjugation. Furthermore, they should deliver their payload to the target site while minimizing their interactions with non-target tissues and organs. In addition, they should have benign chemical characteristics to minimize their toxic effects and enable facile surface modification for delivery to the desired cancerous tissues or organs. Over the years, numerous carriers have been developed for cancer theranostics and related applications, including organic polymers (e.g., polymer microcapsules (Kukkar et al., 2018a), microspheres (MUKERJEE and VISHWANATHA 2009), micelles (Zhang et al., 2014a), dendrimers (Sharma et al., 2017), and liposomes (Riaz et al., 2018)), inorganic materials (e.g., carbon NMs such as carbon nanotubes (CNTs) and graphene) (Hosnedlova et al., 2019), and hybrid NMs (e.g., metal organic frameworks (MOFs) (Jia et al., 2014)). Fig. 2 provides a schematic description of the various categories of nanocarriers used for cancer theranostics.

The organic nanocarriers are the most extensively studied vectors for the delivery of anticancer theranostics. They are typically synthesized by the polymerization and self-assembly of monomeric units. For instance, lipid molecules and amidoamine can self-assemble to yield liposomes and dendrimers, respectively. Therapeutic and imaging molecules can be encapsulated in hollow cavities or nanopores in the polymers to yield nanocapsules or nanospheres, respectively (Fig. 2a and b). Such design architectures are routinely produced using poly ortho esters (e.g., PLGA (Ramazani et al., 2016) and poly (ε-caprolactone) (PCL) polymers (Kamaly et al., 2016)). The outer and inner surfaces of these nanocarriers can be fine-tuned using various emulsification methods to encapsulate both hydrophobic and hydrophilic drugs (Ramazani et al., 2016; Wang et al., 2015b). The presence of pendent functional groups on the surfaces of organic carriers also facilitates the conjugation of imaging and anti-neoplastic drugs for cancer theranostics.

For example, clinically validated PEGylated liposomes were co-encapsulated with a fluorescent probe called indocyanine green and the anticancer drug DOX (Lozano et al., 2015). These theranostic liposomes were further engineered for the surface conjugation of a humanized anti-mucin-1 (MUC1) mAb, hCTM01, to develop a tumor-specific optoacoustic theranostic system. The authors used multispectral optoacoustic tomography to investigate the in vivo uptake of these liposomes in mice bearing fast-growing (4T1) and slow-growing (HT-29) MUC1-positive tumors. The targeted liposomes accumulated rapidly in the periphery of the cancerous tissues, indicating that they were binding to the available MUC-1 receptors (Lozano et al., 2015). On the other hand, non-targeted PEGylated liposomes accumulated slowly in the tumor center at later time points. The study thus depicted the in vivo cancer theranostic potential of a typical organic nanocarrier conjugated with an anti-tumor Ab.

Organic nanocarriers offer many advantages for cancer theranostics: (a) abundant raw materials, (b) facile synthesis, (c) ease of surface modification for combining imaging and targeting ligands, and (d) simultaneous encapsulation of both hydrophobic and hydrophilic therapeutic agents. However, they suffer from some serious drawbacks, such as rapid degradation and elimination in the in vivo environment and undesirable distribution to non-targeted tissues. These limitations reduce the effective drug dosage and explain their unpleasant side effects. Therefore, we next discuss their various types and how they can be used for cancer theranostics upon conjugation with anticancer Abs.

Polymeric nanocarriers offer facile encapsulation of their bioactive components and can also be molded into various morphologies, such as nanocapsules and nanospheres. Their chemical structures can also be tuned for the simultaneous encapsulation and surface conjugation of drug molecules and ligands. As a result, these nanocarriers have been investigated extensively for use in cancer theranostics, and several theranostic systems have been developed using Abs conjugated with synthetic (e.g., PLGA and PCL) or natural (e.g., collagen) polymers. For instance, the CD147 Ab was integrated with spherical α-Hed chitosan nanoparticles (α-Hed-CSNPs) into α-Hed–CS–CD147-NPs (daverage = 148.23 ± 1.75 nm) to investigate their anti-neoplastic activity and cellular uptake in human liver cancer cells (HepG2 and SMMC-7721) (Zhu et al., 2015). Ab-conjugated NPs exhibited lower half-maximum inhibition concentration (IC50) values than free α-Hed and α-Hed-CSNPs. Moreover, the Ab-conjugated NPs showed increased intracellular accumulation of α-Hed–CS–CD147-NPs through the caveolae-dependent pathway. The authors attributed those enhanced characteristics of their NP construct to its strong fluorescence intensity in the tumor environment of nude mice (Zhu et al., 2015). Similar methods for using Ab-conjugated polymeric NPs for cancer targeting have also been described (Feng et al., 2014; Mansur et al., 2015; Zheng et al., 2017). These studies indicate the importance of these nanocarriers for cancer theranostics and related applications.

The versatile chemical structure of polymeric nanocarriers allows them to encapsulate a wide range of therapeutic and imaging agents, including anticancer drugs (e.g., DOX), QDs, and magnetic NPs. The sustained release of the encapsulated content from these nanocarriers under in vivo conditions can occur through numerous mechanisms, including degradation of the polymer wall, drug diffusion through the polymer matrix, and the feedback response of the cell to physicochemical factors such as temperature or pH. Nonetheless, polymeric nanocarriers suffer from many limitations, including a high polydispersity index, rapid degradation, and unreliable release of the payload. These shortcomings can reduce their efficacy in cancer theranostic applications.

Lipid-based nanocarriers have some unique characteristics: (a) relatively high biocompatibility, (b) longevity in a physiological environment, and (c) simultaneous encapsulation of both hydrophobic and hydrophilic drug molecules. These characteristics have propelled the development of lipid-based nanocarriers as a promising platform for cancer theranostics. Lipid-based nanocarriers can be divided into micelles (Fig. 2c), liposomes, solid lipid nanoparticles, nanostructured lipid vehicles, and nanoscale lipid emulsions. Among them, liposomes have been the frontrunner in cancer theranostics, as evidenced by the approval of nearly 15 liposomal drug formulations for clinical purposes (Tang et al., 2018). The typical structure of liposomes comprises bilayer vesicles of natural or synthetic phospholipids enclosing a water droplet or functional molecules (e.g., anticancer drugs and imaging agents) (Fig. 2d). The drug molecules might also be combined with the lipid bilayer, depending on their hydrophilicity or hydrophobicity.

Conventional liposomes are susceptible to rapid clearance by opsonins and the reticuloendothelial system (RES). This shortcoming has prompted further engineering of liposomes using gangliosides (e.g., monosialoganglioside) and PEG molecules to develop stealth liposomes (Alavizadeh et al., 2016). Stealth liposomes offer many benefits for tumor targeting, such as high stability in a biological environment, long circulation periods in biological fluids, the ability to support site-specific ligands on the surface, and good sensitivity toward internal and external stimuli (e.g., enzymes, pH, substrates, and temperature) (Alavizadeh et al., 2016). Long-circulatory stealth liposomes can easily be engineered with Abs or Ab fragments (e.g.,

and single-chain variable fragments (scFv)) to produce immunoliposomes for the targeted delivery of anticancer therapeutics, imaging moieties, and free radical oxygen in radiotherapy. For a detailed discussion of immunoliposomes, see the review by Merino et al. (2018).

Dendrimers are tree-like, exceptionally branched NPs (average particle size <5 nm) with unique structural characteristics (Fig. 2e). They consist of a core with submicronic dimensions for the localization and transportation of therapeutic and imaging agents and numerous surface functional groups with tunable characteristics for combination with the targeting ligands (Myung et al., 2015). Dendritic NPs can be constructed from countless combinations of monomeric building blocks and can be built into a well-defined spherical morphology. Moreover, the extensive branching in dendrimers confers them with a wide range of chemical, biological, and mechanical properties. In comparison to dendrimers, polymeric nanocarriers usually offer limited branching or cross-linking. Consequently, dendrimers provide a potent solution in cancer theranostics. For example, poly(amidoamine) (PAMAM) dendrimers (G4.5) were separately conjugated with two targeting ligands, an interleukin (IL)-6 Ab, and an arginine-glycine-aspartate (RGD) tripeptide to yield G4.5-IL6 and G4.5-RGD conjugates (Fig. 3 A) (Mekuria et al., 2016). The anticancer drug DOX was physically entrapped in the conjugated dendrimers, and then cellular uptake studies were conducted in HeLa cells. Comparative confocal microscopy and flow cytometry analyses of the conjugates revealed that the G4.5-IL6/DOX composite had a lower IC50 than the G4.5-RGD/DOX construct. These observations validated the high drug loading, rapid drug release rate, and high cytotoxic potential of the G4.5-IL6/DOX construct. It was suggested that the G4.5-IL6/DOX composites were rapidly internalized in the cancer cells by means of receptor-mediated endocytosis (Mekuria et al., 2016). Consequently, there was an increase in the late apoptotic/necrotic cell count. Accordingly, the G4.5-IL6 conjugate was recommended as a carrier for the targeted delivery of DOX to cervical cancer cells (Mekuria et al., 2016). A similar report citing the enhanced uptake of a gemcitabine-encapsulated PAMAM dendrimer–Flt-1 Ab conjugate in pancreatic cancer cells was described by Öztürk et al. (2017). Those dendrimer-Ab conjugates accumulated in the pancreatic tumors of nude mice, which confirmed their acceptable in vivo anticancer efficacy (Fig. 3B). Overall, dendrimers are an important carrier type for tracking the progress of cancer metastasis. However, establishing the use of dendrimers in clinical practice will require a significant reduction in the high costs associated with their synthesis.

Inorganic nanocarriers, such as hollow gold nanospheres (You et al., 2010), carbon-based NMs (e.g., CNTs and graphene) (Chen et al., 2015a), silica NPs (Freitas et al., 2017), Ca3(PO4)2 NPs (Tang et al., 2015), and MnO2 NPs (Cho et al., 2017), have also been investigated as carriers for cancer theranostics (Fig. 2f and g). These nanocarriers can be tuned using stimulus-response characteristics (e.g., hyperthermia, light, or magnetic field changes) for the sustained release of anticancer drugs in tumor microenvironments (Lin et al., 2016a). The unique properties of inorganic nanocarriers could help to reverse the effects of MDR protein pumps, which are a common feature of metastatic cancers (Lin et al., 2016a). Moreover, inorganic nanocarriers provide harmonized theranostic effects for cancer treatment because of their ease of synthesis, facile surface modification, inherent imaging function, low polydispersity, and tunable pore properties. However, to develop them into clinical applications, it is essential to overcome their limitations, such as scale-up preparation without any change in quality, biocompatibility, and potential toxicity in in vivo conditions. These challenges require that a substantial database be built to track the nanotoxicity of these systems in vitro and in vivo. Such a database would facilitate the development of more efficient, non-toxic inorganic nanocarriers for clinical practice. The biocompatibility of inorganic nanocarriers can be enhanced by combining them with biocompatible polymers, peptides, and other biomacromolecules. Such hybrid and multifunctional nanocarriers could then be used to improve the treatment of metastatic cancers.

Advances in synthetic chemistry have led to the development of a new category of hybrid nanocarriers that combine the characteristics of both their organic and inorganic precursors to enhance their potential for biomedical applications. One of the most promising examples is MOFs (Fig. 2h), which are constructed by coordinately bridging multidentate organic bridging ligands with metal ions (Kukkar et al., 2018b). Since their discovery in the 1990s, MOFs have been extensively investigated for biological applications, including the delivery of anticancer drugs (Horcajada et al., 2009) and the imaging (Zhou et al., 2018) and biosensing of cancerous tissues (Miller et al., 2016). The numerous unique properties of MOFs, such as their abundant precursors, tunable pores, photoluminescent (PL) emission characteristics, and substantial biocompatibility, recommend them as potential carriers in cancer theranostics.

The simultaneous development of imaging and therapy approaches towards better cancer management has become the need of the hour due to soaring treatment expenses and increasing demand for personalized medicine of cancer. Herein, we briefly review Ab-based various therapeutic and imaging techniques used for cancer treatment.

The immortality and rapid metastasis of cancer cells is conferred by their ability to undergo uncontrolled DNA replication and cell division. Consequently, the design and development of therapeutic approaches has primarily focused on interfering with or inhibiting DNA replication and mitosis in cancer cells (Agudelo et al., 2016; Huang et al., 2017a; Siddik 2005). In addition, many therapeutic drugs target the cytoskeletal components and membranes of cancer cells (Prota et al., 2013). Traditionally, anticancer therapy has been classified into three broad categories: (a) chemotherapy, (b) hormonal therapy, and (c) immunotherapy. Herein we briefly describe the fundamental characteristics of these categories and provide relevant examples.

As a key component of anticancer therapy, chemotherapeutic agents are further subdivided into various components (e.g., alkylating agents, antibiotics, antimetabolites, DNA replication inhibitors, and mitotic inhibitors) (Table 1 ). The basis of those classifications is each agent's chemical structure and mechanism of therapeutic action, as shown in Table 1. Among the various chemotherapeutic drugs, 5-fluorouracil, methotrexate, and 6-mercaptopurine (6-MP) are well-known for their ability to inhibit DNA replication. For instance, 6-MP competitively interferes with the synthesis and metabolism of purine nucleotides in cancerous cells by inhibiting the enzyme phosphoribosyl pyrophosphate amidotransferase (Nielsen et al., 2001). MTX inhibits the catalytic function of the enzyme folic acid (FA) reductase, which catalyzes the conversion of FA to tetrahydrofolate. Tetrahydrofolate acts as a cofactor for the synthesis of nucleic acids and proteins. Thus, the inhibitory effect of MTX leads to the arrest of cell division. Likewise, 5-FU blocks the action of thymidylate synthase, which catalyzes the conversion of deoxyuridine monophosphate (dUMP) into deoxythymidine monophosphate (dTMP). As a result, the synthesis of thymidine (pyrimidine), a monomeric unit of DNA is not accomplished. Consequently, 5-FU treated cancer cells undergo apoptosis due to the scarcity of dTMP. On the other hand, cisplatin blocks DNA replication by interrupting telomerase enzyme activity, which is vital to immortality of cancer cells. The US-FDA approved anthracycline antibiotic-based doxorubicin (DOX) to inhibit the DNA replication process by: (a) intercalating between adjacent nucleic acid bases using its four planar rings and (b) inhibiting the action of the enzyme topoisomerase II through its hydroxyl group. These actions impede the synthesis of DNA and RNA in cancer cells. Apart from those synthetic drugs, various plant alkaloids such as docetaxel, paclitaxel, and flavopiridol have also been extensively investigated for cancer treatment (Fridlender et al., 2015; Gupta et al., 2010). These alkaloids (especially docetaxel and paclitaxel) disrupt the dynamic self-assembly of microtubules to reduce the availability of the free tubulin monomers required for mitotic cell division in cancer cells.

Over the years, many new drug therapies have been developed as alternatives to DNA replication/cell division inhibitors. For instance, anthracycline antibiotics (e.g., DOX and daunorubicin) can interfere with the telomerase activity of cancer cells (Table 1). Telomerase replaces small DNA sequences or telomeres in normal stem cells and cancer cells, allowing cells to divide infinitely without reaching their Hayflick limit. To take advantage of its telomerase inhibitory effect, various commercial dose regimens of DOX have been developed to use encapsulation in nanoscale drug carriers for delivery. For instance, Doxil, a drug regimen of DOX encapsulated in liposomes, was clinically approved and commercialized for the suppression of ovarian cancer and Kaposi's sarcoma (Bulbake et al., 2017).

Hormonal therapy for cancer treatment includes glucocorticoids (GCs), anti-estrogens, and luteinizing hormone releasing hormone (LH–RH) analogs (Table 1). These hormones are generally lipid soluble and preferentially bind to certain regulatory proteins in the cytoplasm or nucleoplasm of cancer cells (Chen et al., 2014b). As representative steroid hormones, GCs have been extensively investigated for about 80 years for use in treating lymphoid tissue malignancies and mitigating chemotherapy-induced side effects (Lin and Wang 2016). Cortisol, for example, is an important biomarker of breast, lung, and prostate cancers (Cheng and Meiser 2019; Schrepf et al., 2013; Sephton et al., 2013). In addition, GCs can be added to chemotherapeutic drugs to induce apoptosis in malignant lymphoid cancers. GCs such as dexamethasone (DEX) are well known to induce apoptotic cell death in lymphoid tissue cancers (Yao et al., 2013). However, the exact mechanism of GC-induced apoptosis is still a major target of research because it involves multiple intracellular signaling cascades (Elmegeed et al., 2016). In addition to GCs, anti-estrogen compounds such as tamoxifen have also been used in breast cancer treatment. Tamoxifen is a non-steroidal triphenylethylene derivative that selectively binds to estrogen receptors on breast cancer cells (Ali et al., 2016; Eeckhoute et al., 2006), which leads to the under-expression of cyclin D1, Myc, Bcl-2, and vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF) proteins responsible for the progression of the cell cycle, cell survival, and the stimulation of angiogenesis in breast cancer cells (Eeckhoute et al., 2006). Likewise, leuprorelin, a synthetic nonapeptide, and Leutinizing hormone-releasing hormone (LH-RH) (a gonadotropin releasing hormone (GnRH) analogue) are also used for breast cancer treatment (Wilson et al., 2007). Leuprorelin binds to LH-RH receptors to stimulate the increased production and release of LH and follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH) from the anterior pituitary (Sharma et al., 2008). As a result, serum estrogen and testosterone levels can increase. However, continuous leuprorelin injection causes desensitization of the LH-RH receptors, which can ultimately suppress LH and FSH levels (Sharma et al., 2008). Consequently, the secretion of estrogen and testosterone is considerably reduced, which can reduce the growth and division of breast and prostate cancer cells (Freites-Martinez et al., 2018; Wilson et al., 2007). During the past 35 years, various other LH-RH analogs, such as goserelin and histrelin, have also been clinically approved by the US-FDA for prostate cancer treatment (Deeks 2010; Ishizuka and Satou 2016; Khemlina et al., 2015).

Chemotherapy and hormonal therapy are conventional and widely adopted forms of cancer treatment. However, these medications suffer from limitations such as an inability to differentiate cancer cells from normal cells, high drug resistance, and low sensitivity. These limitations prompted researchers to develop alternative therapies that use the immune system to counter cancer cells. In this regard, the pioneering “magic bullet” concept of Paul Ehrlich in the early 20th century motivated the development of target-specific therapeutic molecules. Extensive research endeavors have produced mAb-based immunotherapies, which are a primary approach to the specific recognition and inhibition of cancer cells. Immunotherapy is a broad-spectrum approach to induce the host's immune system to produce an enhanced response against cancer (Khalil et al., 2016). Various molecular agents (immunomodulatory molecules (e.g., mAbs) and other regulatory molecules (e.g., interferons, cytokines, and interleukins)) constitute the most recent immunotherapeutic methods. They can be used to provide highly selective treatment for malignant tumors.

Anti-tumor immunotherapy offers advantages over other modes of cancer treatment, including Ab-mediated specific and selective binding to the target molecules and the generation of immunological memory. A perpetual memory response is highly efficient in preventing the recurrence of a malignant tumor, safeguarding against the redevelopment of resistant cancer clones (Khalil et al., 2016). Immunotherapy has been shown to cause rapid and complete eradication of malignant tumors in many cases (Hughes et al., 2016). As such, its efficacy appears comparable or even superior to that of other modes of cancer treatment.

Only in the past decade has anti-tumor immunotherapy been clinically demonstrated to increase the overall survival of patients with advanced-stage cancers. Nonetheless, the fundamental molecular mechanisms that underlie anti-tumor immunotherapy remain incompletely understood. Ab-based immune modulation involves blocking or activating regulatory receptors, which stimulates T-cell activity. These regulatory receptors are critical to tumor cell growth and survival because they modulate intracellular signal transduction mechanisms for the division of cancer cells. Ab-binding to such receptors causes the regression of malignant tumors. Because immunomodulatory mAbs target immune cells rather than a specific cancer cell, they are recognized as a broad-spectrum therapeutic approach for combating different types of malignant tumors.

Immunomodulation with Abs uses two approaches: (a) a checkpoint blockade and (b) T-cell co-stimulation. The former approach restores T-cell activity with mAbs that block their inhibitory receptors, and the latter induces T-cell activity with mAbs that bind to their stimulatory receptors. For instance, programmed cell-death protein 1 (PD-1) is a receptor involved in immune-checkpoint blockade signaling. Inhibition of the PD-1 molecule with nivolumab/pembrolizumab and nivolumab has been tested and approved for treatment of patients suffering from melanoma and non-small-cell lung cancer, respectively.

Targeted immunotherapeutic agents block the mutant protein functions required for the growth of cancer cells (Vanneman and Dranoff 2012). The first report on targeted immunotherapy demonstrated the clinical application of imatinib to treat chronic myeloid leukemia (CML) (O'Brien et al., 2003). Imatinib inhibited the BCR-ABL kinase in CML tumors and thus suppressed them in 76% of patients.

Taking inspiration from the success of imatinib, a clinical trial of ipilimumab was successfully conducted for the treatment of advanced-stage melanoma, which was considered a breakthrough in the development of mAb-based immunotherapy (Hodi et al., 2010). The median lifespan of 676 stage III or IV metastatic melanoma patients treated with ipilimumab increased from 6.4 months to 10 months (Hodi et al., 2010). That 50% increase in the median lifespan of those patients demonstrates the highly potent antitumor effect of this drug. Biochemically, ipilimumab specifically binds to and impairs the activity of cytotoxic T lymphocyte-associated antigen 4 (CTLA4) (O'Day et al., 2007). Consequently, the CTLA4-specific ligands CD80 and CD86 remain active, which results in the regeneration of the activated T cell–mediated immune response and subsequent tumor breakdown (Schadendorf et al., 2015). Ipilimumab-based immunotherapy was the first instance to demonstrate a significant increase in the overall survival time of patients with advanced-stage melanoma in a phase III clinical trial (Hodi et al., 2010). That study also represented a breakthrough in immune-checkpoint modification to improve the survival of patients with advanced-stage cancer post treatment (>2.5 years). In light of the high success rate of ipilimumab, it was approved by the US-FDA for the clinical treatment of patients with advanced melanoma (Hodi et al., 2010). During the past 20 years, various mAbs, such as trastuzumab, pembrolizumab, bevacizumab, nivolumab, and cetuximab, have been approved by the US-FDA for clinical trials (Table 1). The approval of these drugs verifies the success of Ab-based immunotherapy as a substantial alternative to conventional cancer treatments.

For the majority of Abs used for cancer treatment, the regulatory guidelines of US-FDA have been applied to characterize their pharmacokinetic properties, toxicity, and biodistribution profiles (Warram et al., 2014a). Targeted cancer imaging with Abs under clinical settings can be achieved with the aid of their unique features: long half-life (e.g., relative to nanobodies and peptide fragments), low dosage requirements for imaging purposes (e.g., relative to therapeutic applications), non-linear pharmacokinetics, and presence of pendent functional groups for conjugation with imaging agents. The long half-life of Abs (~24 h) facilitates their enhanced uptake in cancerous cells with reduced expression of a specific marker, while the presence of abundant –COOH and –NH2 groups is favorable for the conjugation of Abs with tracing agents. A variety of imaging probes (e.g., magnetic nanoparticles, radioisotopes, fluorophores) can be coupled with Abs for targeted cancer imaging. As a result, Ab-based targeted cancer imaging can be performed by various techniques such as optical imaging, nuclear imaging, MRI, SPECT, and ultrasound imaging (Table 2 ). In the following subsection, we briefly discuss the execution of aforementioned techniques for Ab-based targeted cancer imaging.

Abs conjugated with numerous organic and inorganic fluorophores, and bioluminescent proteins have been reported for cancer imaging. Organic dyes have traditionally been used for the in vivo imaging of biomolecules because of their high photoluminescence intensity and tunable optical characteristics. These dyes can be categorized into two types, resonant dyes and charge-transfer dyes, on the basis of their emission mechanisms. The resonant dyes (e.g., fluorescein isothiocyanate (FITC), rhodamine, and cyanine 5.5 (Cy5.5)) exhibit fluorescence emissions through the delocalization of excited electrons over the aromatic ring. These dyes exhibit relatively narrow absorption and emission bands, small Stokes shifts, high molar extinction coefficients, and moderate-to-high fluorescence quantum yields (Hawe et al., 2008). Charge transfer dyes (e.g., coumarin) are characterized by intramolecular charge transfer transitions between donor and acceptor molecules (Resch-Genger et al., 2008). These dyes have broader absorption and emission bands, larger Stokes shifts, smaller molar extinction coefficients, and lower fluorescence quantum yields (Resch-Genger et al., 2008) than resonant dyes. Organic dyes with visible or ultraviolet (UV) light emissions have low penetration into tissue, which restricts their application to in vitro imaging (He et al., 2018; Hilderbrand and Weissleder 2010), surgery, and endoscopy (Warram et al., 2014a).

On the other hand, NIR-emitting organic dyes offer many unique attributes, such as a tunable chemical structure, higher penetration into tissues, substantial biocompatibility, and minimized interference from the autofluorescence of biological macromolecules (Ren et al., 2018; Zhu et al., 2019b). These properties make NIR-emitting dyes an attractive prospect for cancer diagnosis and phototherapy. However, free organic dyes are water insoluble and readily eliminated from the body by RES organs such as the liver and spleen (Cai et al., 2018). Thus, it is critical to develop protective approaches to enhance the circulation and retention time of organic dyes. Many safeguarding procedures, such as conjugating NIR-emitting dyes with an anti-tumor Ab (Yazaki et al., 2019) or polymeric NPs (Behnke et al., 2013) and encapsulating NIR-emitting dyes in silica NPs (Rampazzo et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2016a) and polymer NPs (Valcourt et al., 2019), have been suggested for cancer theranostics (Table 2).

Likewise, silica NPs doped with iodoacetamide dye NIR-664 (λ ex = 672 and λ em = 694 nm) were tested for fluorescent imaging of colorectal cancer cells (Tiernan et al., 2015). For tumor targeting, the dye-doped NPs were conjugated with humanized anti-CEA mAb A5B7 using dendrimers with each of four linkers (SMCC, PEG, EDC, and PAMAM) in separate experiments. Among the linkers, the NPs conjugated using PAMAM dendrimers showed excellent intracellular uptake in LS174T, LoVo, and HCT116 cells. Hepatic translocation of systemically delivered NPs in murine LS174T xenografts demonstrated their potential for fluorescent in vivo imaging (Tiernan et al., 2015). The MUC1 oncogene is overexpressed in most breast cancer cases, making it an automatic choice for targeted breast cancer imaging. Accordingly, mesoporous silica nanoparticles (MSNs) doped with NIR-797 isothiocyanate dye (λ ex = 795 nm; λ em = 817 nm) were used for in vitro and in vivo optical detection of breast adenocarcinoma tissue (Dréau et al., 2016). The dye molecules were first encapsulated in MSNs, followed by the surface modification of those MSNs with PEG and anti-MUC1 Ab (TAB-004™) molecules in sequential steps to yield the ab-tMUC1-NIR-MSN conjugate. These in vitro studies demonstrated highly specific intracellular uptake of the conjugate (0–500 μg ml−1) in murine mammary epithelial cancer cells MMT and Mtag with negligible cytotoxicity. In vivo studies conducted using non-tumor bearing female C57BL/6 mice demonstrated that there was a substantial localization of the conjugate (16 mg kg−1) in the liver without any acute toxicity (Dréau et al., 2016).

Recently, a PEGylated (sidewinder) anti-carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) mAb (M5A) conjugated with commercial NHS-IRDye800CW was applied for real-time intraoperative image-guided cancer surgery (Yazaki et al., 2019). The anti-CEA M5A-PEG conjugate was characterized by a high dye-to-Ab ratio (average of 7 per Ab). That high loading capacity ensured adequate circulation of the conjugate in the serum and tumor in the in vivo environment (Yazaki et al., 2019). In a human pancreatic cancer mouse model, the conjugate showed increased tumor fluorescence, higher signal to noise ratio, and extended surgical time duration compared with the control. These experimental observations successfully demonstrated the potential of this Ab–dye probe for future clinical applications in CEA-positive gastrointestinal cancers (Yazaki et al., 2019).

Inorganic semiconductor nanocrystals called QDs are an excellent alternative to organic dyes for cell and tissue imaging thanks to their size-dependent sharp and unique emission profiles, outstanding quantum yield, reduced photobleaching, excellent fluorescence intensity, and substantial stability under physiological conditions (Hassan et al., 2018). To reduce the inherent cytotoxicity of conventional QDs prepared using Cd metal atoms, a core-shell approach is adopted to encapsulate those atoms in more benign and biocompatible materials such as Zn (Das et al., 2015) or SiO2 (Tang et al., 2019; Zhou et al., 2017). The emergence of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and amorphous carbon dots (Cdots) has further broadened the spectrum of QDs available for theranostic applications (Ranjbar-Navazi et al., 2019; Zheng et al., 2015). Lv et al. investigated the potential of CuInS/ZnS quantum dots (ZCIS QDs) for multispectral optical tomography imaging and synergistic photodynamic therapy (PDT) in BALB/c nude mice bearing 4T1 tumors (Lv et al., 2016). The ZCIS QDs demonstrated size-dependent theranostic capabilities, with smaller NPs (d = 25 nm) having higher intracellular uptake, longer tumor retentivity, and deeper tumor penetration than larger particles (d = 80 nm). As such, the QDs (λ ex = 660 nm, λ em = 730 nm) exerted simultaneous PTT and PDT effects, showcasing their inherent therapeutic efficacy against tumors in in vivo conditions (Lv et al., 2016). Bao et al. studied the tumor localization of NIR-emitting Cdots (λ ex = 655 nm, λ em = 720 nm) co-doped with sulfur and nitrogen in ICR mice bearing H22 tumor models (Bao et al., 2018). The intravenously injected Cdots accumulated passively in tumor tissue and showed high photothermal efficiency (~59%), excellent optical and photoacoustic imaging capabilities, and reduced clearance via the renal excretion system compared with the controls (mice injected with (i) phosphate buffer saline and laser irradiation and (ii) Cdots without laser irradiation) (Bao et al., 2018). Recently, the performance of hybrid graphene oxide-polyethylene imine-graphene quantum dots (Go-PEI-GQDs) (λ ex = 300 nm, λ em = 425–450 nm) was evaluated for breast cancer theranostics in the MDA-MB-231 cell line by Kumawat et al. (2019). Those authors studied various parameters, such as cell imaging, PTT, and the anti-oxidative stress response, to quantify the performance of the GO-PEI-GQDs. The complexes (50 μg ml−1) showed an excellent PTT response (44–49 °C), with stable fluorescence imaging and excellent cytotoxic effects, indicating their potential for development into a breast cancer treatment (Kumawat et al., 2019).

Like the two photon contrast agents, lanthanide (Ln3+) UCNPs absorb NIR radiation for emission in the visible or UV region with a high anti-Stokes shift (Chen et al., 2014a; Wang et al., 2013). The NIR excitation of UCNPs confers them with certain unique features, such as deep light penetration of tissues, low cytotoxicity, low auto-fluorescence of biomolecules, and outstanding stability in in vivo conditions (Chen et al., 2014a). The upconversion process involves the ensuant absorption of multiple photons with high wavelengths through the ladder-like energy levels of Ln3+ ions to yield short-wavelength photoluminescence (Chien et al., 2018). The theranostic efficacy of a UCNP-based PL analysis can be improved by combining it with other imaging approaches, such as MRI (Tian et al., 2014b), CT (An et al., 2018), SPECT (Cao et al., 2013), PET (Goel et al., 2017), and PDT (Chen et al., 2014a; Qiu et al., 2018).

Many studies have highlighted the potential of UCNP-based immunotherapy for theranostic applications in cancer treatment (Liang et al., 2016; Sang et al., 2019; Shen et al., 2014; Wang et al., 2009). For instance, the practicality of UCNP-mediated PDT was demonstrated in target cancer lines (Liang et al. (2016). The authors prepared silica-coated NPs of green-emitting NaYF4:Yb,Er@NaGdF UCNPs with a silica layer followed by encapsulation of the photosensitizer (PS) rose Bengal (RB). Subsequently, a bifunctional fusion protein (comprising specific binding domains for silica and the Fc fragment of an IgG Ab) was used to prepare a bioconjugate of the NP-RB complex with an anti-tumor Ab. The as-prepared bioconjugate (100 μg mL−1) was further tested (λ ex ~ 980 nm; 1.5 W cm−2) to render the generation of ROS, thereby leading to NIR-induced phototoxicity in human colon adenocarcinoma HT-29 cells under in vitro conditions. As shown in Fig. 4 , the viability of the bioconjugate treated cells was reduced by ~40% after 10 min of irradiation. Increasing the irradiation period (e.g., beyond 10 min) reduced the cell viability below 50%, which demonstrates the efficacy of the prepared bioconjugate for PDT of cancer cells (Liang et al., 2016).

To expand the potential of Ab-based targeted cancer imaging from proof of concept laboratory studies towards human clinical trials, smart optical imaging probes along with state-of-the-art imaging techniques (e.g., photoacoustic imaging (PAI), bioluminescence imaging (BLI), and fluorescence molecular tomography (FMT)) have been developed in the past decade (Dammes and Peer 2020; Kobayashi et al., 2016). (For the detailed information on the above mentioned imaging techniques, readers may refer to the review of Dammes and Peer (2020).) Conventionally used ‘always on’ probes emit detectable signals, irrespective of their proximity with target cells/tissues (Dammes and Peer 2020). As a result, these probes suffer from low target to background ratio (TBR). On the other hand, smart optical imaging probes emit detectable signals only upon binding with the target molecules. These probes are designed using Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) or dimer formation approaches. Some common examples of smart probes for cancer theranostics include – xanthene fluorophores (Li et al., 2017), indocyanine green (ICG) (Wang et al., 2018), matrix metalloproteinase (Zhao et al., 2017), and stimuli responsive compounds (Li et al., 2017; Yang et al., 2019). The conjugation of the aforementioned probes with cancer specific Abs (e.g., anti-HER2, Cetuximab) can substantially improve upon their imaging efficacy of cancerous tissues. Ab-based fluorescent optical imaging of cancers can help surgeons in differentiating the borders of cancerous tissue from the normal tissues (Kobayashi et al., 2016). In addition, it can help in screening of patients with higher expression of cancer specific antigens. Such screening can aid in Ab-based targeted therapies such as Ab-drug conjugates or PTT of cancer tissues.

Nuclear imaging is one of the earliest and most commonly used non-invasive modality for imaging of cancer tissues (Kaur et al., 2012). It employs positron emitting radionuclides, which generate high energy photonic signals to image tumor regions. Depending upon the half-life of radionuclides, nuclear imaging is broadly classified into two types: PET and SPECT. PET imaging employs positron emitting short-lived radionuclides such as 64Cu, 18F, and 15O for generating high-energy photons (Wei et al., 2020). It utilizes positron annihilation from intravenously injected radionuclides for the spatial localization of cancer tissues. Despite excellent sensitivity and high penetration depth, the application of PET scanning is limited due to expensive instrumentation and low spatial resolution. On the other hand, SPECT imaging utilizes radionuclides with longer half-lives such as 111In and 99mTc. SPECT-based imaging offers multiplexed imaging with more economical feasibility as it allows simultaneous usage of multiple probes. However, the application of SPECT has certain limitations such as 10–100 fold reduced sensitivity compared to PET and photon attenuation owing to radionuclide collimators.

Immuno-Positron Emission Tomography (ImmunoPET) is one good example of paradigm shift from conventional PET scanning. It offers excellent specificity for cancer imaging owing to the precised targeting abilities of mAb and enhanced sensitivity/selectivity of PET (Mian and Leslie 2020). A line of clinical evidence indicates the potential of ImmunoPET in the imaging and diagnosis of metastatic complications (Dijkers et al., 2010; Lohrmann et al., 2019). A positive cancer diagnosis using ImmunoPET scanning may facilitate the construction of a roadmap for further treatment regimens. ImmunoPET imaging using various cancer specific antigens (such as receptor tyrosine kinases, CD antigens (e.g., CD20, CD38, CD105, and CD146), carbohydrate antigens, and prostate specific membrane antigen (PSMA), CEA, and carbonic anhydrase) have been described in the literature. A detailed discussion on ImmunoPET and SPECT-based imaging applications of abovementioned biomarkers is provided in Table 2 (Kaur et al., 2012; Wei et al., 2020).

The fundamental principle of MRI involves variation in the magnetic moment of paramagnetic nuclei in the presence of external magnetic field at varying radiofrequency (RF) pulse (Shahbazi-Gahrouei et al., 2019). When the RF impetus is disengaged, the nuclei protons return to the initial energy levels. It is accompanied by transfer of magnetic spin energy to the surrounding tissue, thereby generating a detectable RF response signal. This entire process is referred to as the magnetic relaxation, which leads to the production of a structural image of the tissue. This whole process produces outstanding anatomical information about the tissue. The MRI process offers several advantages for imaging of cancer tissues such as nonionizing radiation, high temporal and spatial resolution, noninvasive imaging, high tissue contrast, and simultaneous provision of anatomical and functional characteristics (Shahbazi-Gahrouei et al., 2019). The sensitivity of MRI can be improved by using contrast agents such as Gd and Gd-encapsulated SiO2 NPs (Zeng et al., 2020), Mn (Samiei Foroushani et al., 2019), SPIONs (Dulińska-Litewka et al., 2019), ultrasmall SPIONs (Du et al., 2020), and UCNPs (Fang et al., 2020). Targeted MRI imaging of cancer tissues can be achieved by conjugating the aforementioned NMs with mAbs as shown in Table 2.

Ultrasound imaging is a real-time and non-invasive modality for screening of cancer tissues. Ultrasound imaging for cancer diagnosis is performed by adding intravascular contrast agents derived from protein/phospholipid shelled microbubbles (1–5 μm) (Warram et al., 2014a). Ab conjugated microbubbles can be specifically used for targeted molecular diagnosis of inflammation and angiogenesis in cancer tissues. Improved vascular internalization of microbubbles was described for surface attached endothelial markers (e.g., anti-vascular endothelial growth factor receptor 2 antibody (anti-VEGFR2 Ab) (Mancini et al., 2013) and P-selectin (Warram et al., 2014b)). Targeted cancer imaging with ultrasonic microbubbles can be synergistically improved by simultaneously conjugating Abs against multiple receptors on cancer cells (Warram et al., 2011). The simultaneous multiple targeting of cancer receptors ameliorates cancer imaging and diagnosis by increasing the probability of identification for the target cells by the microbubbles. Of late, anti-CD146 Ab modified nanobubbles (m146-NBs) were investigated for in vivo imaging of SKOV3 (ovarian cancer cell line) cells inoculated tumor bearing mice models (Hamano et al., 2019) (Table 2). The m146-NBs were prepared by avidin-biotin conjugation approach. Prior to conjugation, the anti-CD146 Ab was modified with protein A/G derived polypeptides which specifically bind to Fc region to achieve enhanced binding of the Ab with human umbilical vein endothelial cells expressing VEGFR-2 receptor. VEGFR-2 is a well-known biomarker of tumor angiogenesis (Pircher et al., 2011). Ultrasound imaging performed by using the contrast harmonic imaging technique revealed prolonged (20 min) contrast images with m146-NBs at the tumor site compared to the pristine nanobubbles (Fig. 5 ) (Hamano et al., 2019). These observations were indicative of higher stability of the m146-NBs at the target tumor vessels (Hamano et al., 2019). As such, the feasibility of ultrasonication-based nanobubbles were demonstrated for applications toward cancer theranostics. The drug delivery capabilities of anti-epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) antibody-conjugated microbubbles (EGFRMBs) were explored by Hirabayashi et al. (2017). These authors used sonoporation approach for enhanced intracellular delivery of bleomycin encapsulated EGGRMBs to human gingival squamous carcinoma Ca9-22 cells. Accordingly, the combinatorial effect of sonoporation and EGFR-MB was suggested to lead to apoptosis mediated strong cytotoxic effect on Ca9-22 cells (Table 2).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Nanomaterials for use in antibody bioconjugates for cancer theranostics

ce:section.ce:para: GNPs exhibit many unique properties (e.g., size-tunable surface plasmon resonance (SPR), ease of synthesis and surface functionalization, high biocompatibility, and low toxicity), which makes them promising candidates for theranostic applications (Wei et al., 2016). For instance, the localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) of GNPs, which represents the oscillation of free electrons at the resonating wavelength (λ = 520–540 nm), helps enhance their light scattering and absorption properties (Lee et al., 2018). Anisotropic GNPs such as gold nanorods (GNRs) have two LSPR bands, in the NIR and visible red region (~520 nm), due to their longitudinal and transverse electronic oscillations, respectively (Huang et al., 2007). The longitudinal absorption maxima in GNRs can be shifted to longer wavelengths as a function of their aspect ratio and is more sensitive to the local dielectric environment than the corresponding LSPR of spherical GNPs. GNRs strongly absorb and scatter electromagnetic radiation and can thus be used as a surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) substrate. Moreover, the NIR absorption of the GNRs minimizes their interference in complex biological environments. As a result, GNRs can be used for in vivo imaging of cancer cells. They can also efficiently absorb x-rays because of their high atomic number and high electron density (Wei et al., 2016). The combined effect of LSPR and strong x-ray absorption makes GNRs suitable for numerous imaging applications, such as dark field microscopy (Ma et al., 2016; Weigel et al., 2014), optical diffraction tomography (Kim et al., 2014), photoacoustic tomography (PAT) (Chen et al., 2019b), and two- or three-photon luminescence imaging (Klekotko et al., 2019; Park et al., 2018). GNRs can methodically transform absorbed light into heat energy, which further expands their potential for PTT (Vines et al., 2019) and thermo-responsive controlled drug release applications (Wang et al., 2019a). Moreover, the presence of pendant surface functionalities on GNPs enables the facile conjugation of biological ligands (e.g., Abs) for targeted delivery applications (Emami et al., 2019).

In light of the aforementioned properties of GNPs and GNRs, many authors have extensively investigated the potential of GNP–Ab conjugates for cancer theranostics, as shown in Table 3 . One pioneering study in this research domain focused on using GNPs as PAT contrast agents (Copland et al., 2004). Those authors conjugated GNPs (average diameter ~ 40 nm) with Herceptin, an anti-HER2 mAb, using streptavidin-biotin conjugation chemistry for PAT imaging of selectively targeted human SK-BR-3 breast cancer cells (Copland et al., 2004). However, the GNPs exhibited an LSPR band at 532 nm, which restricted tissue penetration during PAT imaging.

Gastric cancer (GC) is the second most leading cause of cancer-related deaths worldwide (Sitarz et al., 2018). Gastric cancer stem cells (GCSCs) respond poorly to conventional cancer therapeutic regimes because of their high mutation rate and rapid development of drug resistance. GCSCs are mostly defined by the expression of cell-specific markers that can be transferred as xenografts in experimental animal models. Among the various markers, three molecules, CD24, CD54, and CD44, are expressed in human GC. Specifically, CD44, well known as a transmembrane glycoprotein and surface receptor for hyaluronic acid (HA), is overexpressed in GCSCs. CD44 expression is seminal to the tumor cell characteristics (e.g., survival, progression, and metastasis) that regulate the response of cancer cells to their microenvironment. In light of the attractive characteristics of CD44, PEGylated gold nanostars conjugated with CD44v6 mAbs (CD44v6-GNS-PEG) were synthesized using standard carbodiimide chemistry (Liang et al., 2015). 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) colorimetric assays of the GNS-PEG and GNS-PEG-CD44v6 in GES-1 (normal gastric mucosa epithelial cells) and MKN-45 (GCSCs) showed that they had negligible cytotoxicity, highlighting their excellent biocompatibility (Liang et al., 2015). Furthermore, NIR laser ablation (790 nm, 1.5 W cm−2, 5 min) of the GCSCs containing the conjugate produced complete degradation of the target cells. Photoacoustic imaging of GC xenograft model mice revealed the translocation and accumulation of CD44v6-GNS nanoprobes in the targeted vasculature of subcutaneous tumors within 4 h of injection. Quantitatively expressed, the photoacoustic signals in the test population were enhanced by 250% in comparison to the control population (mice injected with GNS-PEG). Thus, CD44v6-GNS nanoprobes could be an exciting platform for gastric cancer–targeted imaging and PTT (Liang et al., 2015).

Carbon nanomaterials have structural attributes such as delocalized π-electron orbitals, a high surface-area-to-volume ratio, facile molding into 0D (e.g., GQDs), 1D (e.g., CNTs), and 2D nanostructures (e.g., graphene oxide (GO)), high cargo loading capacity, and appreciable biocompatibility (Augustine et al., 2017; Gazzi et al., 2019; Mohammadpour and Majidzadeh-A 2020; Tabish et al., 2018). Carbon nanomaterials can also be fine-tuned to produce highly luminescent nanostructures (e.g., GQDs and Cdots) with sharp emission lines and high photostability in complex biological environments (Boakye-Yiadom et al., 2019; Ko et al., 2017). The presence of oxygen-rich functional groups on carbon nanomaterials and their facile surface modification facilitates their conjugation with hydrophobic drug molecules (Arora et al., 2014), cancer biomarkers (e.g., Abs) (Beckler et al., 2018), aptamers (Cao et al., 2017), peptides (Chen et al., 2019a), and imaging agents (Kumawat et al., 2019). Furthermore, carbon nanomaterials can be made to manifest optical absorption spectra in the NIR or UV region, which enables their application for hyperthermia or PTT of cancer cells (Lu et al., 2019). These features have led to the extensive development of carbon nanomaterials as highly desirable carriers for cancer theranostics and related applications (Maiti et al., 2019). Therefore, the following discussion summarizes the cancer theranostic applications of Ab conjugates prepared with various carbon nanomaterials. The major structural and functional attributes of carbon NM-Ab conjugates are also presented in Table 3.

CNTs, specifically single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs), are a highly efficient theranostic nanomaterial because of their structural and functional attributes: (a) high cargo loading capacity, (b) penetration of cell membranes, (c) unique Raman signals in the NIR region, and (d) active targeting of therapeutic agents and biomarkers (Sanginario et al., 2017; Son et al., 2016). Herein, we describe various studies reporting theranostic applications of CNT–Ab conjugates (Table 3).

In one of the earliest reported studies, SWCNTs were simultaneously functionalized with three compounds (DOX, fluorescein bovine serum albumin (BSA), and an anti-CEA Ab) to yield the formation of BSA@CNT-DOX-anti-CEA (Heister et al., 2009). The CNT-DOX conjugation was achieved by a non-covalent approach in phosphate buffer (pH = 8.5), with a conjugation efficiency of ~87.5%. In contrast, the attachment of fluorescein BSA and the anti-CEA Ab to the CNT-DOX conjugate was performed using the standard carbodiimide approach. Cellular targeting experiments confirmed the dissociation of DOX from the conjugate and subsequent nuclear localization, followed by the internalization of the BSA@CNT-DOX-anti-CEA into WiDr cells. However, fluorescently labeled CNTs were confined to the cytoplasm in all observed cells (100% uptake efficiency) (Heister et al., 2009). Liu et al. (2010) reported multiplexed NIR Raman imaging of breast cancer and colon cancer cell lines using radiolabeled SWCNTs with five different C13/C12 isotope compositions conjugated with four Abs (Erbitux (anti-Her1), Rituxan (anti-CD20), anti-Her2, and anti-CEA) and a peptide cyclic RGD (cyclo-RGDyK). The anti-Her1, anti-Her2, anti-CD20, and anti-CEA Abs were used to target the Her1/EGFR, Her2/neu, CD20, and CEA biomarkers, respectively, and the RGD peptide was used for the recognition of αvβ3 integrin (Liu et al., 2010). Those authors also performed ex vivo Raman imaging on nude mice injected with LS174T colon cancer cells. Interestingly, EGFR expression was considerably upregulated during in vivo tumor expansion, relative to cultured cells. The Raman analysis offered negligible background interference and sharp Raman peaks during the multiplexed analysis of the cultured cancer cells and ex vivo samples. Their efforts were thus the first report of five-color multiplexed molecular imaging of cancer cells using Raman spectroscopy (Liu et al., 2010). In a similar study, an SWCNT-anti-HER2 chicken IgY complex was investigated for the simultaneous sensing and selective photothermal deracination of HER2-positive SK-BR3 breast cancer cells (Xiao et al., 2009). The Raman signal emitted by the SWCNTs was sensitive enough to detect a single SK-BR3 tumor cell in comparison to HER2-negative control MCF-7 cells. NIR irradiation (λ = 808 nm, P = 5.0 W cm−2) of the complex produced a sharp and localized temperature increase of ~14 °C in buffer medium, highlighting its hyperthermia potential. NIR induced photothermal ablation of SWCNT-anti-HER2-targeted SK-BR-3 cells led to a cellular mortality of ~97.7%, whereas the IgY-treated control population suffered only ~5.9% mortality (Xiao et al., 2009).

Cotinine is a physiologically inert and low toxicity (lethal dose50 (LD50) = 4 ± 0.1 g kg−1 in mice) metabolite of nicotine (Kim et al., 2014; Tanner et al., 2015), enabling it to elicit Ab production upon conjugation with a larger carrier molecule. Therefore, Kim et al. (2015) used a cotinine-modified dextran polymer to functionalize SWCNTs. A bispecific anti-HER2 × anti-cotinine tandem ScFv fusion protein was conjugated with the cotinine-functionalized CNTs to yield synthetic CNT/bispecific tandem antibody conjugates (SNAs). Raman imaging of HER2-positive SK-BR-3 cells demonstrated a three-fold enhancement in binding affinity between SNAs and HER2 compared with a CNT–Ab conjugate prepared by carbodiimide chemistry (Kim et al., 2015).

Cancer stem cells (CSCs) are integral to tumorigenic events and play a pivotal role in the progression, resistance, reappearance, and metastasis of this disease (Yan et al., 2015). Thus, it is necessary to develop techniques that simultaneously image and target CSCs to improve cancer management. To this end, a PEGylated SWCNT-antiCD44 nanoconjugate was developed for the multimodal non-invasive imaging of CSCs in xenograft tumor model mice inoculated with MDA-MB-231 human breast adenocarcinoma cells (Faraj et al., 2016).
