The PEGylated SWCNT-anti CD44 complex was also conjugated with various imaging modalities (e.g., superparamagnetic iron oxide nanoparticles (SPION), gallium-67, or a Vivotag-S750 NIR fluorophore) for non-invasive imaging of the tumor-bearing mice. An MTT assay after 72 h of incubation showed excellent biocompatibility of the nanoconjugate, with >90% cell viability for all types of conjugates. MRI, SPECT, and NIR fluorescence imaging revealed preferential bio-distribution of the SWCNTs in RES organs (liver and spleen) 2 and 48 h after injection (Fig. 7 ). The various multimodal imaging modalities described above, along with inductively coupled plasma-mass spectroscopy and immunohistochemistry, demonstrated discrete accumulation of the nanoconjugate in the targeted CD44-rich regions at the tumor site in the mammary fat pad 24 h post injection (Faraj et al., 2016). The authors suggested that their results in this preclinical study were highly motivating for the efficient targeting and treatment of breast CSCs. The theranostic performance of iron oxide nanoparticles attached to SWCNT-CD-105-DOX conjugates was investigated using luciferase (Luc2)-expressing 4T1 (4T1-Luc2) murine breast cancer cells (Al Faraj et al., 2014). Cell viability assays (TiterTACS™ Colorimetric Apoptosis Detection Kit, poly (ADP-ribose) polymerase, and thiobarbituric acid-reactive substances assays) with the SWCNT-CD105-DOX complexes revealed a considerable increase in apoptosis, DNA damage, and oxidative stress relative to non-conjugated SWCNTs (p < 0.05). These observations distinctly support the substantial biocompatibility of SWCNTs. Bioluminescence imaging (BLI) of 4T1-Luc2 cells over 2–72 h unequivocally demonstrated a higher decrease in photon flux or radiance efficiency at various concentrations (2.5–10 μg ml−1) of SWCNT-DOX/SWCNT-CD-105-DOX relative to DOX alone. These observations indicate that DOX has increased therapeutic efficacy upon conjugation with SWCNTs. Non-invasive BLI and MRI of the primary tumor sites in xenograft nude mice revealed initial tumor growth 3 weeks after the injection of tumor cells. However, one-month post injection of magnetic NP-coated SWCNT-CD105-DOX and SWCNT-CD105, the tumor size was attenuated and metastasis was inhibited (Al Faraj et al., 2014).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Theranostic applications for other cancer types
ce:section.ce:para:
The uptake and internalization of an SWCNT-integrin α
v
β 3 conjugate in U87MG human glioblastoma cancer cells was described by Ou et al. (2009). The conjugate was efficiently internalized in the target U87MG cells, with negligible toxicity and low uptake efficiency in integrin α
v
β 3-negative MCF-7 cells (Ou et al., 2009). An important histological feature of glioblastoma is the presence of unique CSCs called CD133+ cells, which cause tumor recurrence. In this respect, an SWCNT-anti-CD133 mAb conjugate was investigated for its ability to target and photothermally ablate CD133+ cells in a cellular mixture containing CD133- cells as well. (Wang et al., 2011). NIR irradiation and localized hyperthermia of the cellular mixture led to the selective destruction of CD133+ cells. Moreover, xenotransplantation of anti-CD133-SWCNT-treated CD133+ cells led to the loss of tumor sustainability in nude mice upon NIR irradiation. This study furnished a proof of concept to exemplify the potential of SWCNT–Ab conjugates for targeting and PTT of glioblastoma CSCs under both in vitro and in vivo conditions (Wang et al., 2011).

Angiogenic vessels are an important target when developing therapeutic strategies for cancer. In this regard, E4G10 (IgG type) co-appended as covalent complexes of SWCNTs were prepared with chelates of radioactive actinium (225Ac)-labeled 1,4,7,10-tetraazacyclododecane-1,4,7,10-tetraacetic acid (DOTA) and radioactive zirconium (89Zr)-labeled desferrioxamine B (DFO) in separate experiments (Ruggiero et al., 2010). The prepared complexes were called SWCNT-([225Ac] DOTA)(E4G10) (Construct I) and SWCNT-([89Zr]DFO)(E4G10) (Construct II). Construct I and Construct II were applied to radioimmunotherapy (RIT) and radioimmunoimaging, respectively, of tumor vasculature in human colon adenocarcinoma cells (LS174T) grafted into mice. 225Ac is already a clinically approved therapeutic moiety used to treat cancer, and 89Zr is used for in vivo immuno-PET imaging of cancerous tissues. On the other hand, the E4G10 Ab specifically recognizes the monomeric vascular endothelial-cadherin (VE-cad) epitope expressed on angiogenic vessels in human colon adenocarcinoma (LS174T) (Ruggiero et al., 2010). Those authors achieved conjugation efficiency of 3 IgG molecules per CNT for both Construct I and Construct II. The presence of either of the radiolabeled components in combination with the E4G10 Ab substantially reduced the tumor volume and increased the blood compartment clearance kinetics in comparison to conjugates prepared using only the Ab. The RIT data with Construct I showed that the median survival rate of the xenograft mice increased to 26 days, with shrinkage in the tumor volume after 10 days. PET imaging with Construct II displayed rapid blood clearance (<1 h) and specific tumor accumulation of the conjugate in the tumor vessels of the murine LS174T xenograft model (Ruggiero et al., 2010). Those authors thus successfully demonstrated the bimodal imaging and therapeutic efficacy of their SWCNT carrier that targets tumor blood vessels.

In spite of the well-versed structural and functional attributes of CNTs for cancer theranostics, it is highly challenging to observe their imaging and therapeutic effects at the level of a single nanotube. To do that, an anti-EGR Ab, EGFR, GNPs, and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) co-immobilized onto magneto-fluorescent multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) were conjugated with DOX in the presence of the cross-linker genipin (GP) (Zhang et al., 2017). The as-prepared GP-GdN@CQD-MWCNT/DOX-EGFR complex was used for dual modal imaging, along with PTT and chemotherapy for A549 (lung cancer) and MDA-MB-231 cells (breast cancer) in living mice. In vitro drug release observations suggested that 4 h of NIR triggered a 15% release of DOX from the complex in a mildly acidic environment (pH 5.0). These observations indicate stimulus-response drug release behavior, which is ideal for the ablation of cancerous tissues. The use of a dot blotting method confirmed EGFR overexpression in A549 and MDA-MB-231 cells, which were compared with EGFR-negative H522 and MCF-10A cells (Fig. 8 A and B). The A549 cells incubated with GP-GdN@CQD-MWCNT/DOX-EGFR were characterized by distinct localization of this complex and uniformly apportioned, intense red fluorescence (Fig. 8C and D). Observations from in vitro cell-viability assays (Calcein AM (green) and PI (red) co-staining method) suggested acceptable cell viability in cell populations treated with a laser in buffer, which exemplified the inefficacy of the solution in generating hyperthermia for cancer cell ablation. On the other hand, A549 and MDA-MB-231 cells incubated with the complex were characterized by low viability counts (~10%) upon NIR irradiation, indicating simultaneous DOX release and heat generation. Thus, the GP-GdN@CQD-MWCNT/DOX-EGFR complex could be used as a dual chemo/PTT agent to kill cancer cells (Zhang et al., 2017). In vivo photothermal imaging studies of the complex-treated mice illustrated its local hyperthermic effects (temperature = 51.8 °C) relative to the control groups. Moreover, the mice that received the GP-GdN@CQD-MWCNT/DOX-EGFR complex and NIR irradiation were characterized by complete eradication of the tumor after 14 days. This can be ascribed to the synergistic chemo/PTT and enhanced EPR effect (Zhang et al., 2017). In addition, the GP-GdN@CQD-MWCNT/DOX-EGFR complex caused negligible toxicity to non-targeted organs such as the spleen, kidney, lung, and heart. Therefore, those authors suggested that the GP-GdN@CQDs-MWCNTs/DOX-EGFR complex is a potent theranostic platform for treating malignancies.

Compared with NIR radiation, microwaves offer deeper penetration of tissues and instantaneous local hyperthermia when used with CNTs (Vázquez and Prato 2009). Therefore, Beckler et al. (2018) used an MWCNT-anti-CD44 conjugate as a microwave absorber to target PC3 prostate cancer cells. CLSM observations showed that the MWCNT-anti-CD44 conjugates adhered to the cell membrane after a 1 h incubation. The authors suggested that microwave heating of the MWCNT-anti-CD44 conjugates caused rupturing of the cell membrane and thereby cell death. The heating rate of the conjugates (heat irradiated from the MWCNTs and dispersed in the surrounding medium) increased by 27% at a 0.2 mg ml−1 concentration of MWCNTs and microwave power of 900 W. In vitro cell culturing experiments with the MTT assay yielded >20% cell death. At 900 W, an increase in irradiation time from 7 to 8 s (power output = 7200 J) produced a substantial decrease in cell viability, from 83% to 51% (Beckler et al., 2018). Thus, a 900 W power output and MWCNT concentration of <0.1 mg ml−1 were suggested as optimized conditions for microwave treatment of PC3 prostate cancer cells.

Insulin-like growth factor receptor (IGF-R) is a well-known biomarker used to detect pancreatic cancer cells because it is seminal to the division, differentiation, recurrence, and metastasis of cancerous cells (Mutgan et al., 2018). Specifically, IGF-1R, a transmembrane protein on the stromal regions of pancreatic cancer, is a specific target for developing anticancer therapies because its inhibition can substantially block tumor growth and tumor vasculature (Ireland et al., 2016). To this end, cyanine7 (CY7) was appended to PEGylated SWCNT-anti-IGF-1R conjugates to inhibit the downstream signal transduction pathway by blocking IGF-1R and enable PTT of orthotropic pancreatic cancer (BXPC-3 and PANC-1 cells) (Lu et al., 2019). The SWCNT-CY7-anti-IGF-1R conjugates demonstrated excellent NIR absorption and emissions (λ ex = 746 nm, λ em = 770 nm) and photothermal conversion efficiency (increase in temperature from 25 to 60 °C over an irradiation time of 5 min), indicating their outstanding potential for NIR targeting in cancer theranostics. Cell counting kit-8 (CCK-8) assays, apoptotic assays (e.g., Calcein-AM), and ROS tests were conducted on various pancreatic cancer cell lines (ASPC-1, BXPC-3, PANC-1, and SW1990) exposed to the SWCNT-CY7-anti-IGF-1R conjugates. The results showed negligible toxicity of the nanotubes or their conjugates, thereby indicating that these nanocarriers have excellent biocompatibility. In vivo optically guided PTT studies showed increases in the temperature of the SWNT-CY7-IGF-1R treated group of up to 48.53 ± 1.38 °C over 5 min of laser irradiation. The median survival rate of the SWNT-CY7-IGF-1R-treated group was ~38 days. Moreover, the treated animals showed increased body weight compared with the experimental controls, suggesting the low toxicity of the CNTs and their conjugates. PTT using the CNT-anti-IGF-1R had excellent therapeutic effects with minimal toxicity; thus these conjugates are a promising approach to cancer theranostics (Lu et al., 2019).

In the past decade, a major research area in cancer theranostics has been the development of photosensitizers with improved light penetration potential in live cells to enable efficient imaging and PDT (Tabish et al., 2018). For this effort, graphene, along with its various modified forms (e.g., GO and reduced graphene oxide (rGO)), offers unique physicochemical features, such as high surface area (~2630 m2 g−1), delocalized π orbitals, high optical transparency (~2.3% of incident light absorbed by a single graphene layer), inherent NIR absorption and emissions, high fluorescence quantum yield, appreciable mechanical strength (Young's modulus ~ 1100 GPa), high thermal conductivity (~5000 W m−1 ·K−1), ability to immobilize biomolecules, substantial biocompatibility with low toxicity, and a high ROS-generation yield (Gazzi et al., 2019; Novoselov et al., 2012; Orecchioni et al., 2015; Sur 2012; Tabish et al., 2018). These attractive properties have inspired the development of graphene–Ab conjugates as highly formidable cancer theranostic tools. Herein we describe the performance of those conjugates from a clinical perspective (refer to Table 3 for the major structural features of graphene-Ab conjugates).

HER2 overexpression occurs in most cases of metastatic breast cancer. Molecular imaging of HER2 is usually performed using a radiolabeled trastuzumab (Tz) mAb. However, this Ab has unusually long systemic circulation, which can yield low signal-to-noise ratios during cancer detection (Stern 2012). Knowing this limitation of Tz, Cornelissen et al. (2013) hypothesized that 111In-BnDTPA-NGO-Tz immunoconjugates (BnDTPA = 2-(4-aminobenzyl)-diethylenetriaminepentaacetic acid; NGO = nanographene oxide) could confer a pharmacokinetic profile conducive to real-time imaging of HER2-overexpressing tumors. BnDTPA, a metal ion chelator, was conjugated with NGO via ππ-stacking, followed by radiolabeling with 111In to yield 111In-BnDTPA-ππ-NGO (111In-NGO). Subsequently, 111In-NGO was conjugated with Tz using the carbodiimide method to yield 111In-NGO-Tz complexes. SPECT imaging was performed to evaluate tumor accumulation of the 111In-NGO-Tz complexes in two mouse models: (i) BALB/c nu/nu mice with 231/H2N xenografts (test population) and (ii) BALB/c neuT mice (control population). The uptake of the 111In-NGO-Tz complex in the test and control populations 72 h post injection was 15.0 ± 3.7% and 12.7 ± 0.67% of the injected dose·g−1 (%ID·g−1) of tumor tissue, respectively (Fig. 9 A). The uptake of the complex in the test population was substantially higher than that in the non-specific control (111In-NGO-IgG ~0.11 ± 0.04 %ID·g−1) (Fig. 9B). In the presence of excess Tz, the amount of 111In-BnDTPA-ππ-NGO-Tz localized in the tumor was substantially reduced, to 1.45 ± 0.04 %ID·g−1, because the HER2 receptor was blocked. These observations indicate that the 111In-NGO-Tz complex was rapidly cleared from systemic circulation, and the tumor translocation and uptake of the complex were specifically mediated by the interaction between Tz and HER2 (Cornelissen et al., 2013). This study thus demonstrated a promising application of NGO for developing radiolabeled nano-immunoconjugates for cancer theranostics.

Angiogenic biomarkers are an important molecular tool in cancer theranostics research because of their critical role in the induction, growth, and metastasis of cancer (Zhao and Adjei 2015). Developing theranostic devices that actively target angiogenic biomarkers is an important area of research because those devices will not require extravasation into the tumor tissues. Among the various angiogenic biomarkers, follicle stimulating hormone receptor (FSHR), a G-protein-coupled transmembrane protein, is abundantly and specifically expressed within the tumor vasculature of various types of cancer and metastatic sites (Radu et al., 2010; Siraj et al., 2013). Furthermore, FSHR is expressed at a relatively low rate in healthy tissues, which makes this protein a highly specific angiogenic biomarker. Therefore, Yang et al. (2016) studied the synthesis of an anti-FSHR mAb-GO conjugate for in vitro, in vivo, and ex vivo imaging and targeting of breast cancer metastasis. PEGylated GO was treated with 2-S-(4-isothiocyanatobenzyl)-1,4,7-triazacyclononane-1,4,7-triacetic acid) (p–SCN–Bn-NOTA) or FITC to yield NOTA-GO-FSHR-mAb or fluorescein-GO-FSHR-mAb, respectively. The FSHR targeting potential of the fluorescein-GO-FSHR-mAb conjugate was evaluated on click beetle green luciferase–transfected MDA-MB-231 (cbgLuc-MDA-MB-231) breast cancer cells (FSHR positive) and SKOV-3 cells (FSHR-negative). A flow cytometry analysis and CLSM studies revealed enhanced fluorescence intensity in the cbgLuc-MDA-MB-231 cells relative to the SKOV-3 cells. PET imaging of 64Cu-labeled NOTA-GO and NOTA-GO-FSHR-mAb conjugates over a 24 h incubation period demonstrated that >91% of the 64Cu was retained on the GO conjugates. These observations indicate the excellent stability of these 64Cu-labeled GO conjugates. In vivo pharmacokinetic studies of cbgLuc-MDAMB-231 tumor-bearing mice revealed that the circulation half-life of the 64Cu-NOTA-GO-PEG-FSHR-mAb was 3.5 ± 1.7 h. Tumor accumulation of the 64Cu-NOTA-GO-FSHR-mAb at 0.5, 2, 4, and 24 h post injection was 10.6 ± 1.1, 10.6 ± 1.0, 11.6 ± 1.1, 9.3 ± 0.9% ID·g−1, respectively, indicating excellent tumor uptake and stability. In addition to the tumor uptake, the conjugate also accumulated in the liver (15–18 %ID·g−1 over 0.5–24 h) over the course of the study. In comparison to the 64Cu-NOTAGO-FSHR-mAb conjugates, the non-specific conjugate (64Cu-NOTA-GO) showed poor accumulation (1.9–3.8% ID·g−1) in cbgLuc-MDA-MB-231 nodules. Histological investigations of the NOTA-GO-FSHR-mAb verified its preferential accumulation in tumor vasculature over the liver and spleen (Yang et al., 2016). The DOX loading capacity of the GO-FSHR-mAb conjugate was 756 mg g−1, confirming its potential as a drug carrier. DOX-loaded GO-FSHR-mAb conjugates also showed preferential accumulation in cbgLuc-MDA-MB-231 metastatic sites, verifying their potential for use as a targeted drug delivery system (Yang et al., 2016).

Endoglin (CD105) is an exceedingly potent tumor angiogenesis marker associated with low diagnostic rates and poor prognosis in several solid tumor types (Dallas et al., 2008). Thus, it is important for cancer theranostics. Considering the clinical significance of CD105, Cai and coworkers reported that GO and anti-CD105 (TRC105 IgG type mAb) covalent conjugates had in vivo tumor neovasculature imaging and targeting potential for circulating tumor endothelial cells (Hong et al., 2012). Four different conjugates of PEGylated GO were prepared in that study to achieve the desired objectives. Two of the PEG–GO conjugates were further functionalized with NOTA, a64Cu quencher, to yield the NOTA-PEG-GO and NOTA-PEG-GO-TRC105 conjugates. These conjugates were further labeled with 64Cu to conduct non-invasive serial PET imaging and targeting in tumor-bearing mice. Two other fluorescent conjugates (FITC-GO and FITC-GO-TRC105) were prepared in separate experiments (Hong et al., 2012). These conjugates were investigated for a comparative in vitro evaluation of their CD105 binding affinity and specificity in human umbilical vein endothelial cells (HUVECs, CD105 positive) and MCF-7 human breast cancer cells (CD105 negative). Fluorescence and flow cytometry analyses revealed that FITC-GO-TRC105 had strong and specific binding efficacy toward the CD105 expressed by the HUVECs in comparison to the control conditions (FITC-GO and MCF-7). PET scanning and biodistribution studies revealed that the localization of the 64Cu-NOTA-PEG-GO-TRC105 conjugate was higher in the tumor vasculature of 4T1 tumor-bearing mice than in all their major organs except the liver, spleen, and blood. On a comparative note, the 4T1, liver, and spleen uptake of 64Cu-NOTA-PEG-GO was negligible, confirming the CD105 targeting ability of the TRC105 Ab. Overall, the results of the in vivo and ex vivo experiments also showed negligible localization of the conjugate in non-target tissues, indicating negligible extravasation of the conjugates from the tumor site (Hong et al., 2012). Taken together, the experimental observations indicate that CD105 is a promising vascular biomarker that deserves further research in cancer theranostics. The same research group also reported the synthesis and theranostic application of the 64Cu-NOTA-rGO-TRC105 conjugate to in vivo tumor vasculature imaging and targeting in 4T1 tumor-bearing mice (Shi et al., 2013). PET imaging revealed satisfactory stability and a target-specific biodistribution profile of the conjugate both in vitro and in vivo (Shi et al., 2013). Thus, both studies demonstrated the potential of GO–Ab conjugates as excellent carriers for cancer theranostics.

As described in section 4.1, uniformly dispersed and spatially confined GNPs are a highly sensitive and efficient SERS substrate to enhance Raman signals. However, in solution form, GNPs are highly susceptible to aggregation, which reduces the intensity of Raman signals (Tian et al., 2014a). Immobilizing GNPs over mesoporous nanomaterials (e.g., GO (Lin et al., 2016b) or silica NPs (Lee et al., 2012)) has been suggested as a way to enhance their stability. Stable and intense Raman signals produced from GO–GNP nanocomposites endows them with theranostic capabilities and enhanced therapeutic efficacy. To this end, an anti-EGFR-PEG-rGO@CPSS-GNP-R6G (CPSS = carbon porous silica nanosheet) nanocomposite was prepared by self-assembly of GNPs encapsulated in CPSS nanochannels on an rGO surface, followed by covalent attachment of R6G and an anti-EGFR Ab on the GNP surface (Chen et al., 2016d). The prepared nanocomposite combined the photosensitive properties of rGO, plasmonic properties of GNPs, cancer targeting of the anti-EGFR Ab, and cellular uptake enhancement and defined orientation capabilities of NIR-absorbing GNPs and CPSS in a single platform. As such, this multifunctional nanocomposite displayed excellent cancer theranostic capabilities. A SERS analysis revealed that the rGO@CPSS-GNP-R6G induced a 5 × 106 fold increase in the 1510 cm−1 Raman signals characteristic of the R6G molecule (detection limit ~ 10−6 M R6G). The enhanced SERS effect of the anti-EGFR-PEG-rGO@CPSS-GNP-R6G was ascribed to the large surface area of the rGO and the SPR coupled with the SERS hot-spots generated between the dense GNPs spatially confined within the cylindrical channel pores of the rGO@CPSS-6-Au. The anti-EGFR-PEG-rGO@CPSS-GNP-R6G nanocomposite was thus used to selectively target EGFR-overexpressing A549 lung cancer cells. NIR irradiation (0.5 W cm−2) of the target cells yielded high PTT efficiency because of the synergy between the conjugated GNPs and rGO nanosheets (Chen et al., 2016d). The observations experimentally demonstrated the excellent cancer theranostic potential of this low-power-consuming anti-EGFR-PEG-rGO@CPSS-GNP-R6G nanocomposite, which had non-invasive cell targeting, cell tracking, and PTT capabilities (Chen et al., 2016d).

Subcellular organelles such as mitochondria are central to cellular functioning and maintenance because of their energy generation capabilities. Specifically, mitochondria play an important role in controlling apoptosis. Thus, designing and fabricating drug delivery devices that target tumor mitochondria could be a valuable cancer theranostics approach (Farsinejad et al., 2015). Accordingly, a tumor and mitochondria two-step-targeted drug delivery system composed of a pyropheophorbide-a (PPa)-NGO-integrin αvβ3 mAb nanoconjugate with PDT and PTT competence was developed as a phototoxicity on/off switching system (Fig. 10 a) (Wei et al., 2016). The synthesis process used PEGylation of the water-insoluble photosensitive PPa, followed by its covalent immobilization over the NGO surface (Fig. 10b). The authors expected that upon light irradiation, the excited PPa would transfer its emission energy to the GO through ππ interactions. Due to that confined interaction, the authors further suspected that the corresponding Fourier Resonance Energy Transfer mechanism would quench the PPa fluorescence, turning the nanoconjugate to a ‘phototoxic switch off’ state. However, in polar aprotic solvents such as DMSO, the fluorescence intensity of PPa was restored through disruption of the ππ interaction between the PPa and NGO (Wei et al., 2016). As a result, the PPa-NGO-integrin αvβ3 mAb nanoconjugate turned to its ‘phototoxic switch on’ state. Given that photosensitive on/off switching possibility, the nanoconjugate was proposed for in vivo tumor targeting and imaging of integrin αvβ3 positive U87 cancer cells. A confocal microscopy analysis of the nanoconjugate suggested that the αvβ3 receptor guided tumor accumulation in U87 cells, which produced time-dependent mitochondrial accumulation (80% at 8 h). In addition, the observations also manifested the photosensitive ‘turn on’ switch condition for the nanoconjugate and mitochondria as the main tumor targeted organelle. Following mitochondrial targeting and the addition of DMSO, the nanoconjugate turned ‘on’ and induced apoptosis (Wei et al., 2016). A CCK-8 assay on the U87-MG cells demonstrated that the PPa-NGO-mAb nanoconjugate had substantial biocompatibility. A quantitative analysis of the cytotoxicity assay revealed a 70% loss in viability of U87 cells during 5 min of laser irradiation (30 J cm−2). On the other hand, the PDT cytotoxicity of the nanoconjugate to MCF-7 cells was negligible. The enhanced cytotoxic effect of the PPa-NGO-anti integrin αvβ3 mAb in U87MG cells can be attributed to the synergistic effect between the conjugate's drug loading capacity and the mAb-guided specific cell targeting (Wei et al., 2016). Recently, a GO-triphenylphosphine-magnetic Fe3O4-folic acid (GFFP-TPPa) nanoconjugate was investigated for its mitochondrial targeting ability in HeLa cells (Zhang et al., 2020). Overall, subcellular targeting is an exciting domain for PDT-based treatment of cancer cells.

GQDs are heterogeneous, fluorescent carbon nanocrystals prepared by oxidizing GO (Sammi et al., 2018), exfoliating graphite NPs (Zhou et al., 2016), or pyrolyzing organic molecules such as glucose (Gu et al., 2014) and citric acid (Naik et al., 2017). Unlike conventional inorganic semiconductor QDs, the inherent structural defects of GQDs enable the control of their opto-electronic properties (Schroeder et al., 2016; Sheng et al., 2012). The presence of abundant oxygen-containing functional groups facilitates the immobilization of biomolecules such as Abs across the surfaces of GQDs. Such bio-conjugated GQDs have attracted extensive attraction for bioimaging and targeted drug delivery applications (Schroeder et al., 2016). Similar to GQDs, Cdots are zero-dimensional fluorescent carbon nanomaterials with many advantageous properties: excellent photostability, negligible photoblinking, tunable excitation and emission spectra, appreciable aqueous solubility, multitudinous surface functionalities (e.g., amino, carboxyl, or hydroxyl), low toxicity, and economical synthesis routes (Peng et al., 2017; Tuerhong et al., 2017; Wang et al., 2019c). Therefore, various research groups have investigated the cancer theranostic capabilities of Cdot-based bioconjugates in cellular or in vivo environments (Ge et al. 2015, 2016; Huang et al., 2012). Given the excellent fluorescent and bioconjugation properties of GQDs and Cdots, we here discuss various studies of their Ab conjugates for cancer theranostic applications (Table 3).

The anti-HER2 Ab is used extensively for breast cancer treatment because of its inhibition of Her2 and subsequent apoptotic death of cancer cells (Wong and Hurvitz 2014). However, this drug suffers from low anti-tumor efficiency, which requires that it be combined with other cancer treatment regimens (Tolaney et al., 2015). An anti-HER2 labeled GQD-layered protonated titanate (LPT) nanoflower (anti-HER2@GQD-LPT) conjugate was proposed as a multifunctional fluorescence imaging and targeted drug delivery platform (Zheng et al., 2013). The prepared GQDs were characterized by an intense blue emission profile (λ ex = 488 nm, λ em = 520 nm), and the LPT nanoflowers were shown to have a high DOX loading capacity (~9.74%). The inherent fluorescence of the GQDs and presence of the anti-HER2 Ab were expected to give the anti-HER2@GQD-LPT nanoconjugate improved cellular uptake and intracellular tracking capabilities compared with the GQD–LPT conjugate used as the control. According to a fluorescence microscopy analysis, endocytic uptake of the DOX-loaded anti-HER2@GQD-LPT nanoconjugate was observed in HER2-overexpressing MCF7 cells (Zheng et al., 2013). CLSM studies showed improved nuclear localization of the DOX, confirming the DNA targeting capability of the nanoconjugate. The IC50 concentrations of free DOX, DOX-loaded GQD–LPT, and DOX labeled anti-HER2-GQD–LPT were ~0.7, ~0.2, and ~0.1 μg ml−1, respectively. The low IC50 value of the nanoconjugate suggests a synergistic effect of anti-HER2 labeling, which improved its targeting specificity and anticancer efficacy (Zheng et al., 2013). Overall, the authors reported the anti-HER2–GQD–LPT nanoconjugate as an encouraging platform for cancer theranostics.

A nanoconjugate containing GQDs, an anti-HER2 Ab, and β-cyclodextrin (β-CD) was developed for theranostic profiling of breast cancer cells (Ko et al., 2017). Blue-emitting GQDs (λ ex = 370 nm, λ em = 450 nm) were prepared by the pyrolysis of L-glutamic acid (as a precursor), followed by the sequential attachment of β-CD and the anti-HER2 Ab. The β-CD-GQD-HER2 conjugate was further loaded with DOX using a host–guest interaction between the β-CD and DOX molecules. A CCK-8 assay showed that the β-CD-GQD-HER2 conjugate substantially increased the viability of HER2-positive BT-474 cells (from <5% to >35%) as the conjugate concentration increased (from 20 to 500 μg ml−1), confirming the breast cancer targeting ability and excellent biocompatibility of the β-CD-GQD-HER2 conjugate. DOX immobilized on the conjugate was able to release 70% of the drug within 28 h at pH 5.5 and 37 °C. This dual stimulus profile for responsive release demonstrates the targetability of this nanoconjugate to cancer tissues. CLSM-assisted cellular uptake studies revealed the cytosolic and lysosomal localization of the β-CD-GQD conjugate and DOX molecules, respectively, in BT-474 cells. Accordingly, rapid release of DOX from the β-CD-GQD-HER2 conjugate is anticipated upon intracellular uptake. Moreover, the β-CD-GQD-HER2-DOX conjugate (100 μg ml−1) reduced the viability of BT-474 cells to <30%, indicating its excellent anti-proliferative effect on cancer cells (Ko et al., 2017). In a similar study, an Erbitux (cetuximab)@GQD conjugate was prepared for the targeted imaging of EGFR-overexpressing squamous cell carcinoma cells and an in vivo animal model (Jia et al., 2017).

Recently, covalent conjugates of GQDs with a recombinant scFv EGFR Ab were prepared via amide linkages (GQDs-scFvB10) (Nasrollahi et al., 2019). The low molecular weight of the scFv (25–30 kDa) relative to the anti-EGFR mAb (160 kDa) enhanced its cellular uptake efficiency by reducing the steric hindrance during interaction with the cell membranes. A cisplatin (CDDP)-loaded GQD-scFv conjugate (CDDP@GQD-scFvB10) was also found to have high loading capacity (~50%) and a stimulus-response (pH) release profile. Moreover, the CDDP@GQD-scFvB10 nanoconjugate reduced the viability of the targeted MDA-MB-231 cells by ~25% compared to the CDDP@GQD nanoconjugate. Thus, scFvB10 thus apparently promotes the cellular uptake of CDDP@GQDs (Nasrollahi et al., 2019).

During a CT scan, compounds made of high atomic-number elements (e.g., iodine and barium) are known to increase the contrast of the scanned tissue in comparison to the surrounding tissue (Lusic and Grinstaff 2013). The diagnostic accuracy of the conventional CT method can thus be improved by incorporating iodinated compounds (Lee et al., 2013b). However, iodinated compounds can also reduce the performance of CT scans through non-specific biodistribution and low blood circulation time, and they can also cause kidney damage at elevated concentrations and other physiological disorders (e.g., bradycardia and vasodilation) (Lusic and Grinstaff 2013). Encapsulating iodinated compounds within a nanocarrier or conjugating them with a nanopolymer could remediate those shortcomings (Cormode et al., 2014). In this regard, a one-pot synthesis method was adopted to realize the formation of iodine-doped carbon dots (I-Cdots) (λ ex = 380 nm, λ em = 430 nm) and investigate their potential as high-contrast CT imaging agents (Su et al., 2018). The as-prepared I-Cdots were highly biocompatible (e.g., low toxic effects on HeLa cells at 500 μg ml−1). The I-Cdots were further conjugated with cetuximab in an EDC-NHS catalyzed reaction to yield I-CQD-C225. An MTT assay across different cell lines, HCC827 (an EGFR-overexpressing non-small lung cancer (NSLC) cell line), H23 (NSLC cell line with low EGFR expression), and HLF (normal lung cells with a regular EGFR expression profile), revealed that the I-CD-C225 had low toxicity (80% cell viability) across 100–400 μg ml−1 (Su et al., 2018). A CLSM analysis showed the appreciable targeting ability of the I-CD-C225 in the form of a bright fluorescence image of EGFR overexpression in HCC827 cells.

Ab-directed magnetic iron oxide nanoparticles (IONPs) and SPIONs offer unique features as potential cancer theranostic agents, such as their inert chemistry, high drug-loading capacity, low systemic toxicity, biocompatibility, biodegradability, and production of intense and stable imaging signals in clinical conditions (Singh and Sahoo 2014; Zhu et al., 2017). Because crystalline SPIONs composed of iron or its oxide possess unique superparamagnetic characteristics, these materials can be used as MRI contrast agents (Zhu et al., 2017). Herein we summarize some recently reported studies about the cancer theranostic applications of IONP–Ab conjugates (Table 3).

Active targeting of SPIONs can bypass the RES organs and enhance the EPR effect, which promotes high therapeutic efficacy. In this regard, 111In-labeled PEGylated SPIONs were conjugated with trastuzumab and DOX for targeting and dual modal imaging of SK-BR3 breast cancer cells (Zolata et al., 2015). DOX-conjugated SPIONs displayed a pH-dependent drug release profile (10% and 44% at pH 7.4 and 4.6, respectively), supporting their potential as a controlled release vehicle for tumor therapy. In vivo biodistribution studies in BALB/c mice bearing HER2+ breast tumors demonstrated trastuzumab-induced preferential localization of the SPIONs in the tumor tissues, with minimal observations in the non-specific organs. SPECT and MRI scanning of the tumor region displayed distinct contrast in the coronal SPECT images (compared to the control specimens) from 4 to 48 h post injection (Fig. 11 A). These observations indicate the Ab-directed tumor uptake of the SPIONs (Zolata et al., 2015).

The in vitro MRI performance of a deimmunized mouse mAb J591 conjugated with Molday ION rhodamine-B carboxyl (MIRB) was investigated for the non-invasive sensing of prostate-specific membrane antigen (PSMA) (Bates et al., 2014). The elemental iron content in the conjugate was calculated to be 1958 ± 61 iron atoms per Ab. Immunofluorescence microscopy and flow cytometry studies illustrated the subcellular and extracellular localization of the J591 Ab in PSMA+ LNCaP cells. On the other hand, the J591-MIRB conjugate showed negligible fluorescence in the control cell line (DU145), supporting its specificity to prostate cancer cells. The annexin V assay results indicated that the conjugate had a pro-apoptotic effect on LNCaP cells. These observations confirmed the anticancer properties (e.g., inhibition of cellular proliferation and promotion of apoptosis) of the conjugate. In vitro MRI measurements (400 MHz) showed a substantial reduction in T2 relaxation time for the J591-MIRB conjugate (1669 ± 59 ms) compared with the non-conjugated Ab (1865 ± 78 ms). The T2 relaxation time for the conjugate-treated LNCaP cells declined in a linear manner as the Ab concentration increased from 0.01 to 0.28 μg μl−1. The faster MR scanning property of the J591-MIRB conjugate and its specificity toward PSMA + LNCaP cells supports its potential as a highly specific theranostic agent for MRI of prostate cancer cells (Bates et al., 2014).

A combination therapy of cetuximab and FOLFIRI (5-fluorouracil, leucovorin, and irinotecan) has been approved by the US-FDA for targeted treatment of EGFR+ metastatic colorectal cancer (Van Cutsem et al., 2011). To this end, the periodate oxidation method was used to form a conjugate between dextran-coated PEGylated SPIONs and cetuximab to yield cet-PEG-dexSPIONs (Tseng et al., 2015). On average, 31 ± 2 cetuximab molecules were immobilized per SPION. Enzyme linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) and immunofluorescence assays showed the specific attachment of cet-PEG-dexSPIONs to EGFR+ A431 human epidermoid carcinoma cells. In vitro Alamar Blue cytotoxicity studies in a human foreskin fibroblast (Hs68) cell line demonstrated that the SPIONs had low cytotoxicity (cell viability >90%), even at a very high iron concentration (~100 μg ml−1). Moreover, serum-starved A431 cells showed substantial impediment of EGF-induced EGFR phosphorylation in the presence of the cet-PEG-dexSPIONs. Phosphorylation was thoroughly inhibited in the presence of the cet-PEG-dexSPIONs at 50 μg ml−1 equivalent iron concentration. Furthermore, cetuximab-treated cells underwent internalization of EGFR, followed by the suppressive expression of the membrane EGFR protein and a decrease in cellular EGFR levels. The conjugate also produced considerable enhancement in apoptosis-induced death among EGFR+ A431 and 32D/EGFR cells relative to cells treated only with cetuximab (Tseng et al., 2015). Those results thus demonstrate the cancer targeting and therapeutic potential of the cet-PEG-dexSPIONs toward EGFR-expressing tumors.

Non-fouling surface coatings can substantially reduce the aggregation of NPs and their non-specific binding with serum proteins under ex vivo conditions (Chen et al., 2017; Wu et al., 2013). Therefore, lipid-encapsulated SPIONs were coated with cetuximab for the recognition of EGFR-positive glioblastomas in non-invasive T2 MRI assays (Chen et al., 2017). Flow cytometry studies with cetuximab-SPION conjugates revealed their specific targeting of EGFR-positive glioblastoma cell lines (U-251 and DBTRG-05MG) compared to U-87 cells, which have low EGFR expression. These observations were further substantiated by the Immunofluorescence labeling method, in which intense green fluorescent signals were observed specifically from U-251 cells (Chen et al., 2017). The targeting efficiency of the synthesized EGFR-SPIONs was also verified using Prussian blue staining of glioblastoma cell lines with differential EGFR expression profiles (Fig. 11B). Furthermore, the T2 MR relaxation time of the EGFR-SPIONs could be used to recognize EGFR-overexpressing U-251 cells. This observation also helped to distinguish U-251 cells from U-87MG cells, which have low EFGR expression. These findings reveal that the lipid-encapsulated EGFR-SPIONs offer specific and differential targeting of three EGFR-expressing glioblastoma cell lines and could thus be used for non-invasive molecular MRI of tumor tissues (Chen et al., 2017).

VEGF, a cytokine, is overexpressed in both benign and metastatic cancer cells (e.g., breast, cervical, colon, lymphoma, and ovarian cancer cells) (Delli Carpini et al., 2010). VEGF is well recognized as a targeting molecule for the treatment of angiogenesis in breast cancer (Chekhonin et al., 2013). Most VEGF targeting approaches have failed through the development of anti-angiogenic, resistant signal transduction pathways in tumor cells (Zhao and Adjei 2015). Nonetheless, VEGF is still used as a target when developing anti-angiogenic therapies because of its high expression in tumor cells. Thus, an MNP-anti-VEGF-mAb nanoconjugate was loaded with DOX to enhance attachment to the murine breast adenocarcinoma 4T1 cell line and increase the translocation of DOX to the tumor tissue (Semkina et al., 2018). The calculated blood half-life of the conjugates was ~14.6 h. Intravenous injection of these nanoconjugates led to a 50% increase in the mean survival rate of mice bearing 4T1 tumors. Also, MRI of the 4T1 tumors showed an accumulation of the drug-loaded nanoconjugates in tumors 24 h after injection (Semkina et al., 2018).

Recently, CD44 Ab-conjugated magnetic-fluorescent iron-oxide carbon hybrid nanomaterials (MCNPs) were described by Han et al. (2019). The as-synthesized MCNPs had characteristic blue emissions (λ ex = 369 nm, λ em = 425 nm, quantum yield ~58.4%) with strong excitation wavelength–dependent fluorescence characteristics. The T2 relaxation time of the MCNPs was substantially lower than that of pristine IONPs at similar iron concentrations. This observation suggested a significant increase in the transverse proton relaxation process of the MCNPs. The signal intensity corresponding to the T2 relaxation time was considerable reduced in MCNP-CD44-treated 4T1 cells compared with cells treated with MCNPs alone. Fluorescence imaging studies of the MCNP-CD44 conjugate showed excitation wavelength–dependent bright blue, green, and red fluorescence emissions from 4T1 cells. These observations confirmed the fluorescence emission characteristics of the MCNPs as a function of the excitation wavelength. Moreover, fluorescence spectroscopy studies confirmed the ability of the MCNP-CD44 conjugate to selectively distinguish 4T1 cells from MCF-10A cells in a cellular mixture. The MCNP-CD44 conjugate had a highly specific dual fluorescence/MRI contrast effect on 4T1 breast cancer cells. The as-prepared conjugate was able to selectively distinguish the overexpressed CD44 molecules on the 4T1 cells with high affinity and sensitivity (up to a few hundred cancer cells) (Han et al., 2019). Those authors thus showed that their multifunctional MCNP-CD44 conjugates had sensitive and selective cellular-level resolution in the MRI-based diagnosis of breast cancer cells.

Semiconductor nanocrystals or QDs are quantum confined fluorescent nanomaterials (average diameter 2–10 nm) with distinctive optical and electronic properties (Gao et al., 2004). Some notable QDs are derived by combining elements from groups II and VI (e.g., CdSe, CdS, CdTe, ZnO, ZnS, ZnSe, ZnTe, PbSe, and PbS), groups III–V (e.g., GaAs, InP, GaP, and GaN), and group IV (e.g., C, Si, and Ge) (Gao et al., 2004; Tripathi et al., 2015). The most remarkable features of QDs are size-dependent optical emission profiles, narrow emission bands, broad excitation spectra, and resistance to photobleaching (Gao et al., 2004). These structural features enable QDs to perform multiplexed optical detection of genes, proteins, and small-molecule libraries. Moreover, their size-tunable optical properties allow the fabrication of NIR-emitting QDs (Kumar et al., 2019). In in vivo imaging applications, NIR QDs exhibit greater tissue penetration depth than QDs with emissions in the UV/visible region (Ma et al., 2019b). However, QDs made with Cd or Se cores are known for their toxicity (Oh et al., 2016). To cope with that limitation, several surface modification approaches (such as silanization, ligand exchange, coating with amphiphilic polymers, and biomolecule conjugation) have been suggested to improve the biocompatibility of Cd- and Se-based QDs (Liu et al., 2017; Oh et al., 2016). Those approaches not only improve the aqueous stability of QDs but also reduce their toxicity. Such surface-modified or bio-conjugated QDs have been used as exceptional optical probes for cancer theranostics. The following section summarizes various studies of QD-Ab bioconjugates in cancer theranostic applications (Table 3).

NIR-emitting InP/ZnS QDs are known to exhibit lower toxicity than CdSe or CdTe QDs (Brunetti et al., 2013). Accordingly, the theranostic value of these QDs was tested against triple negative breast cancer (TNBC) by preparing a conjugate with amphiphilic block copolymer polylactide-b-poly (ethylene glycol) (PLA-PEG) (Wang et al., 2017). The QD-PLA-PEG micelles were encapsulated with the anticancer flavonoid aminoflavone (AF), followed by surface immobilization of TNBC-targeted anti-EGFR nanobody (Nb) 7D12 to yield anti-EGFR Nb 7D12@InP/ZnS-PLA-PEG QD@AF micelles, which exhibited moderately improved cellular uptake and cytotoxicity in EGFR-overexpressing MDA-MB-468 TNBC cells relative to micelles without Nb conjugation. In addition, the micelles also reduced tumors more efficiently than the control in an orthotopic TNBC xenograft mouse model without causing any systemic toxicity. As such, the QD-based Nb-conjugated micelles could be a compelling theranostic platform against EGFR-overexpressing cancers (Wang et al., 2017).

ELISA is a gold standard for cancer diagnosis (Zhang et al., 2019a). However, it suffers from several shortcomings, such as prolonged duration and the ability to detect only a single analyte per test (Brazhnik et al., 2015). A multiplexed detection approach could substantially improve the sensitivity and selectivity of cancer diagnosis. Sukhanova and coworkers contributed substantially to that end by developing a QD-based microarray detection approach with multiplexed cancer biomarkers (Brazhnik et al., 2015; Nifontova et al. 2018, 2019; Sukhanova et al., 2016). In a pioneering study of the multiplexed, quantitative detection of cancer, an immunodiagnostic lab-on-a-bead microarray was developed using melamine formaldehyde (MF) polymer beads coated with QDs that detect prostate cancer markers in human serum samples (Brazhnik et al., 2015). Specifically, CdSe/ZnS QDs emitting both yellow (λ em and 515 nm) and orange (λ em and 581 nm) spectra were attached to the surface of MF particles (4.08 μm). Positively charged poly(acrylic acid) green and orange emitting QD microbeads were separately immobilized with mAbs specific to free prostate specific antigen (PSA) and total PSA, respectively. The PSA-positive samples yielded fluorescence transfer from the shorter wavelength (orange) PE-Cy5-A-negative samples to the longer wavelength (red) PE-Cy5-A-positive samples in the presence of a mixture of free- and total-PSA-specific beads. Comparatively, no fluorescence shift was observed in negative control samples containing PSA-form-specific microbeads in a buffer solution or serum. The limit of detection for the analysis of free and total PSA concentrations was 0.067 and 0.12 ng ml−1, respectively. The prepared assay was efficient in terms of short run time, low analysis cost, and appreciable compatibility with classical flow cytometers (Brazhnik et al., 2015). Later, Bilan et al. (2017) developed a QD-encoded fluorescent microsphere (QDEM)-based triplex (3-plex) immunoassay for the concomitant detection of lung cancer markers (LCMs) in clinical samples of bronchoalveolar lavage fluid (BALF). For this purpose, MF resin microspheres of different sizes (4.08, 6.1, and 8.24 μm) were separately labeled with PEG-functionalized CdSe/ZnS QDs (λ em = 515 nm). The as-prepared QDEMs were separately functionalized with three Fab fragments of three mAbs against three different LCMs (α-1-microglobulin/bikunin precursor (AMBP), peroxiredoxin 2 (PRDX2), and Parkinson disease protein 7 (PARK7)). The QDEM-Fab conjugate was added to BALF, followed by sequential mixing of biotinylated detection antibody and streptavidin-R-phycoerythrin (SAPE). The qualitative and quantitative estimation of individual LCMs in the BALF samples was performed using variations in the fluorescence intensity of the QDs and SAPE. Preclinical validation of the single and 3-plex QDEM immunoassays using flow cytometry was performed on BALF samples collected from 42 lung cancer patients and 10 control subjects. Higher concentrations of the AMBP (3.4 fold) and PRDX2 (2.0 fold) markers were observed in the lung cancer group than in the control group. Contrarily, the PARK7 concentration was 0.49 times lower in the test subjects than in the control subjects. The QDEM-based results were compared with the gold standard Luminex xMAP® technology, which also yielded increases in the AMBP (5.30 fold) and PRDX2 (2.62 fold) concentrations and decreases in the PARK7 (by 0.36 fold) concentration in cancer patients relative to the control subjects. These observations validated the efficacy of the QDEM immunoassay observations in terms of providing a clear discrimination between control and clinical samples (Bilan et al., 2017). Recently, this research group further assessed the cytotoxicity of CdSe/ZnS QDs (λ em = 590 nm) coated with CaCO3 microcapsules to HER2-positive SK-BR3 human breast carcinoma cells (Nifontova et al., 2019). The QD-coated microcapsules produced 20% cell viability (IC20 = 37.4–44.4 microcapsules/cell) over an incubation period of 48–72 h. Comparatively, the placebo (QD-free) microcapsules produced only negligible cytotoxicity, confirming the toxic effects of the QDs. Moreover, the same authors used trastuzumab-functionalized QD-microbeads for real-time targeting of BT-474 human breast ductal carcinoma cells. The trastuzumab-QD-microcapsule conjugates containing 30 and 300 μg of the Ab exhibited enhanced binding capacity to BT-474 cells relative to conjugates with 3 μg of the Ab. The study thus demonstrated the potential of Ab-microcapsule conjugates for sensitive and selective targeting of live cancer cells (Nifontova et al., 2019).

Apart from various chemically synthesized NMs, biological or bio-inspired NMs (e.g., exosomes, microvesicles, and solid lipid NPs) are also known for their immense potential in cancer theranostics. These NMs mimic the biological membranes in terms of uni- or bi-lamellar and laterally arranged lipid layers with an aqueous internal environment (Kastelowitz and Yin 2014; Madamsetty et al., 2019). Consequently, biological NMs offer several merits (e.g., high biocompatibility, low toxicity, biodegradability, facile synthesis, and encapsulation of both hydrophilic and hydrophobic compounds) over chemically synthesized NMs (Madamsetty et al., 2019). Exosomes are submicron extracellular vesicles, which participate in inter-cellular communication. In addition to normal cells, cancer cells also secrete exosomes. Cancer-derived exosomes are suggested to play a regulatory role in cancer progression and immunity against cancer cells (Zhang and Yu 2019). As a result, these extracellular vesicles can serve as carriers for delivery of diverse array of cargo materials such as nucleic acids, proteins, lipids, and signaling molecules. In consideration of these properties, we hereby discuss some recent reports on targeted delivery of various anti-cancer drug molecules using biological NM-Ab conjugates.

Emtansine (DM1) is a US-FDA approved anti-cancer medication owing to its ability to inhibit microtubule assembly in HER2+ breast cancer cells. DM1 can be specifically delivered to these cells upon conjugation with the mAb Trastuzumab (T) (Barok et al., 2011). Binding of T-DM1 to HER2 receptors on cancer cells elicits receptor-mediated endocytosis of the T-DM1-HER2 complex, followed by the intra-lysosomal degradation of trastuzumab. The remaining DM1 molecules are released into the cytosol of cancer cells. In light of this discussion, Barok et al. (2018) investigated the anti-cancer potential of T-DM1 conjugated over the surface of exosomes derived from HER2+ breast and gastric cancer cells. Exosomes were isolated from HER2+ (breast cancer cells (SKBR-3 and EFM-192A) and gastric cancer cells (SNU-216)) and HER2- MCF-7 breast cancer cells, followed by exposure to T-DM1. Free T-DM1 was removed using HER2-coated magnetic beads. Qualitative and quantitative verification of T-DM1 coated exosomes were performed by electron microscopy, flow cytometry, and Western blotting techniques (Barok et al., 2018). Confocal microscopy studies revealed the binding of T-DM1-coated exosomes to cancer cells and subsequent internalization of T-DM1 complex. It was observed that T-DM1 was bound exclusively to exosomes derived from HER2-positive cancer cells. Alamar Blue test revealed substantial decrease in the viability of SKBR-3 and SNU-216 cells treated with T-DM1 coated HER2+ exosomes (Barok et al., 2018). Caspase-Glo 3/7 caspase activation assay confirmed the inhibition of HER2+ SKBR-3 cells exposed to T-DM1-containg exosomes along with activation of caspases 3 and/or 7. The results suggested the activation of apoptosis induced cell death through mediation by exosomes derived from HER2+ cancer (Barok et al., 2018). These authors claimed their study as the first report on the apoptotic effects of exosome-bound anti-HER2 agents on cancer cell.

Nanobodies are small (~95kD) single variable domains derived from heavy-chain antibodies from Camelidae species (Oliveira et al., 2013). Nanobodies offer several advantages compared to conventional antibodies such as recombinant production along with high chemical and thermal stability. In this regard, extracellular vesicles (EVs) producing neuro2A cells were transfected with pLNCX-DAF-R2 or pLNCX-DAF-EGa1 vectors (DAF = decay-accelerating factor) encoding for anti-EGFR nanobodies – R2 and EGa1 (Kooijmans et al., 2016a). The R2 nanobody (control nanobody) binds specifically to the azo-dye Reactive Red (RR6) whereas the EGa1 nanobody exhibits higher affinity for EGFR (compared to the natural ligand epidermal growth factor (EGF)). The nanobodies were expressed over EVs surface upon conjugation with the C-terminal glycosylphosphatidylinositol (GPI) peptide derived from human DAF. The DAF peptide is carved off by the catalytic action of GPI transamidase enzyme, thereby facilitating nanobody immobilization over GPI anchors in cells (Kooijmans et al., 2016a). This way, nanobodies would localize to GPI-rich lipid rafts in EV membranes (Kooijmans et al., 2016a). Flow cytometry analysis revealed successful expression of GPI-linked nanobodies over EVs’ surfaces compared to EVs from control cells. GPI-linked nanobodies displayed on EVs did not cause any variation in the characteristics (e.g., morphology, size distribution, and protein marker expression) of EVs. Further, the EVs exhibited improved binding to EGFR expressing A431 tumor cells under flow conditions (Kooijmans et al., 2016a). These authors provided experimental proof for expression and anchoring of GPI conjugated nanobodies on the surface of EVs.

Exosomes are reported to hold immense potential as carriers for the delivery of various therapeutic agents (e.g., nucleic acids, chemotherapeutic drugs and enzymes) to facilitate the treatment of cancers in animal models (Wang et al., 2020b). Nonetheless, pristine exosomes suffer from numerous drawbacks such as low yield (Goh et al., 2017), inferior purity grade (Nakai et al., 2016), low specificity for cancer cells (Kooijmans et al., 2016b), and rapid plasma clearance under in vivo conditions (Kooijmans et al., 2016b). To overcome these limitations, Si et al. (2020) developed a high yield and scalable (0.5–5 L) method using HEK-293 cells for producing exosomes. In vitro characterization of the exosomes using electron microscopy, nanoparticle tracking analysis, and western blotting confirmed their high purity and production yield. Besides, flow cytometry and confocal laser scanning microscopy were demonstrated to have the ability of prepared exosomes to tag neuroendocrine cancer surface specific (SSTR2) targeted anti-somatostatin receptor 2 (anti-SSTR2) mAb for cancer targeting. The targeted drug delivery ability of the anti-SSTR2-exosome complex was validated by encapsulation of a histone deacetylase inhibitor called romidepsin (FK228) into their core. In vitro anti-cancer toxicity study showed low viability (17.9 ± 0.9%) of mAb-exosome-FK228 over human pancreatic neuroendocrine tumor cancer cell lines (BON cancer cells). In vivo tumor targeted studies conducted over BON-Luc subcutaneous mice confirmed the cancer targeting specificity of PEG-mAb-exosome-Cy7 complex. Likewise, the targeted imaging studies conducted using cyanine7-labeled mAb (mAb-Cy7) verified their successful localization in the tumor tissue of mice xenograft tumor model (Si et al., 2020).

In addition to the abovementioned NMs, many other types of NMs (e.g., silica NMs and UCNPs) have also been conjugated with Abs for cancer theranostic applications (Table 3). Specifically, silica and silicon dioxide have been listed under the ‘generally recognized as safe’ category of materials by the US-FDA. During the past few years, scientists have made unprecedented efforts to develop silica-based hybrid NMs for targeted cancer theranostics (Kesse et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2015a). As a result, the silica-shell-coating method has become one of the most extensively used, economically viable, and practical options for the design and development of hybrid NMs (Table 3). In this regard, a theranostic core-shell nanocomposite system (CuS@MSN) was built by encapsulating water-soluble CuS NPs with biocompatible MSNs (Chen et al., 2015b). The CuS NPs (average diameter ~11 nm) are strong NIR absorbers with a high molar extinction coefficient (8.66 × 107 cm−1 M−1 @ 930 nm) (Zhou et al., 2010). The surface of the CuS@MSNs was maneuvered with amino groups (-NH2), NOTA, PEG, TRC105, and the positron emitter 64Cu (Chen et al., 2015b). The DOX loading capacity of the CuS@MSNs was 465.1 mg g−1 over a 24 h incubation period. DOX-loaded CuS@MSN core-shell nanocomposites exhibited a rapid release profile under acidic conditions (pH 5) (Chen et al., 2015b). The pH values of tumor cells (lysosomes: pH 4–5) and the tumor microenvironment (pH 6.5–7.2) are considerably lower than those of normal tissues (pH 7.4). Therefore, this observation indicated their stimulus-response release property. The results of an NIR light absorption (980 nm, 4 W cm−2, and 5 min duration) analysis of the CuS@MSNs indicated that 100% of light absorption took place during a temperature rise of up to 25 °C (Chen et al., 2015b). These observations indicate the high efficiency of the CuS@MSN core-shell NPs in converting energy from light to heat. In in vivo photothermal ablation studies, treating 4T1 tumor-bearing mice with CuS@MSNs caused a substantial increase in the temperature of the tumor tissues (from 25 to >42 °C) upon irradiation with an NIR laser (980 nm, 4 W cm−2, 15 min). Note that complete disappearance of tumors from the NIR irradiated mice within two weeks suggests the high efficiency of such PTT. An analysis of CD105+ HUVECs treated with NHS-fluorescein@CuS@MSN-TRC105 indicated a considerable increase in the mean fluorescence intensity of the HUVECs. On the other hand, cells treated with fluorescein-CuS@MSNs were characterized by a minimal increase in fluorescence intensity. These observations confirmed the in vitro CD105 targeting ability of the nanoconjugate toward breast cancer cells. Likewise, PEGylated CuS@MSNs (dose ~90 mg/kg) were intravenously injected into healthy female BALB/c mice (weight: ~18 g, n = 3). Those mice showed negligible changes in their histological patterns and body weights compared to the control group, confirming the biocompatibility of the nanoconjugates. In vivo tumor vasculature–targeted PET imaging and biodistribution studies with 64Cu–CuS@MSN-TRC105 nanoconjugates in 4T1 murine breast tumor–bearing mice showed a maximum accumulation of 6.0 ± 0.4% ID·g−1 24 h post injection, which was considerably higher than in the control group (1% ID·g−1) injected with only 64Cu–CuS@MSNs. The biodistribution profiling of the nanoconjugates indicated that they had dominant liver uptake under ex vivo conditions (Chen et al., 2015b). Evidence collected from various in vitro and in vivo targeting and PTT approaches clearly demonstrates the potential of CuS@MSN core-shell NPs for cancer theranostic applications.

Recently 89Zr-radiolabeled, TRC105-conjugated, hybrid and hollow mesoporous silica nanoparticles (HMSNs) were reported for in vivo tumor vasculature targeting in 4T1 murine breast tumor–bearing mice (Chen et al., 2018a). Three types of inorganic nanoparticles of varying morphologies and sizes (oleic acid stabilized UCNPs, SPIONs, and QDs) were encapsulated into single HMSNs to form core-shell hybrid NMs. Specifically, mice with 4T1 tumors underwent PET imaging with 89Zr-labeled and TRC105-conjugated UCNP@[89Zr]HMSN-PEG5k-TRC105 HMSNs. Those mice exhibited rapid clearance of the HMSNs from systemic circulation (half-life ~4.5 h) and accumulation in RES organs (Chen et al., 2018a). The maximum accumulation of the UCNP@[89Zr]HMSN-PEG5k-TRC105 in the mice with 4T1 tumors was 10.0 ± 0.9% ID·g−1 at 6 h post injection (Chen et al., 2018a). This strategy could be applied to the synthesis of other types of core-shell theranostic NMs for in vivo tumor-targeted imaging and drug delivery.

Hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) is a type of cancer with a high metastasis and mortality rate. It is usually less responsive than other cancers to conventional chemotherapy and radiotherapy treatment regimens (Tunissiolli et al., 2017). Therefore, it is imperative to develop more sensitive and effective diagnostic and treatment methods for this disease. In this regard, NaYF4:Yb,Er@NaGdF4 UCNPs (λ ex = 980 nm, λ em = 650 nm) were surface conjugated with anti-epithelial cell adhesion molecule (EpCAM) Abs and mitoxantrone (MX) (a photosensitizing anti-tumor drug) (Han et al., 2018). The resulting anti-EpCAM-UPG micelles were highly biocompatible and non-toxic to macrophage cells and healthy mice at 1200 μg ml−1 and 40 mg ml−1, respectively. Upon NIR irradiation (980 nm laser, 320 W cm−2, and 10 min) of HCC cells treated with the anti-EpCAM-UPG micelles, the rapid release rate of MX (82 wt% of the drug released over 30 h) promoted the killing of the HCC cells. CLSM studies of the anti-EpCAM-UPG micelles revealed high green intensity in the cytosol of the treated BEL-7404 cells (human liver cancer cell line). The viability of the HCC cells treated with anti-EpCAM-UPG micelles was reduced by 94%, whereas the viability of cells exposed to pure MX plus laser irradiation and pure MX alone was 80.6% and 75.9%, respectively. The substantially upgraded therapeutic performance in the targeted cells (relative to the non-targeted groups) was ascribed to the Ab-mediated enhanced cellular uptake of the micelles, along with the synergetic chemotherapy and PDT effect of the UCNPs and MX. In vivo experiments demonstrated the improved magnetic resonance/upconversion luminescence (MR/UCL) imaging performance of the targeted micelles compared with the non-targeted micelles (Han et al., 2018). The UPG micelles had outstanding anti-tumor properties and offered simultaneous dual-modality MR/UCL imaging, good biocompatibility, and negligible toxicity in the diagnosis and treatment of HCC.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Challenges to the use of Ab–NM bioconjugates for cancer theranostics
ce:section.ce:para: Theranostic approaches offer a great blueprint for cancer treatment through the synergistic effects of therapeutic and detection modalities. In this regard, Ab–NM conjugates are an exciting means of cancer treatment thanks to the high affinity and specificity of Ab molecules for cancer biomarkers. On the other side, theranostic NMs offer many unique properties (e.g., high biocompatibility, enhanced solubility, prolonged systemic circulation, and penetration of biological barriers) to enable active targeting of cancer tissues (Lim et al., 2015). As such, various advanced materials such as UCNPs, MNPs, GNPs, and QDs have been devised to offer excellent, highly sensitive imaging attributes for cancer diagnosis. Furthermore, numerous polymeric nanocarriers, such as PLGA, have proven to be excellent cargo carriers (Ghitman et al., 2020).

In spite of the great achievements already made in the field of Ab–NM-based theranostic agents, many challenges remain unresolved, such as finding the precise quantification and appropriate orientation of Abs on NMs without compromising their antigenic specificity, stability, off-site toxicity, and high synthesis cost of both Abs and NMs. A major objective in various Ab–NM conjugation methods is how to minimize the loss of Ab specificity caused by the necessary engineering processes. Although several covalent and non-covalent approaches have been used to obtain the desired orientation of the Ab on the NM, it is difficult to ignore the chances of ending up with a non-favorable orientation (Bartczak and Kanaras 2011; Hermanson 2013; Jazayeri et al., 2016; Kumar et al., 2008; Mustafaoglu et al., 2017; Perez et al., 2004; van der Meer et al., 2017; Wang et al., 2002). For instance, the utility of GQD–Ab conjugates for cancer theranostics has been scantily reported, presumably because of limitations such as their structural heterogeneity, low quantum yield, and broad spectral lines. Variations in the Ab orientation and the number of Abs attached to NMs can significantly alter the theranostic performance during clinical applications. Likewise, the quantification and orientation of the therapeutic agent is equally important in developing an efficient theranostic agent. Furthermore, murine Abs are characterized by a short half-life and minimal interactions with human immune cells (Arruebo et al., 2009). In cases in which an additional contrast or imaging agent is required, their relative quantities must also be optimized. For such applications, using NMs that have separate binding sites for the therapeutic agent, contrast/imaging agent (if required), and targeting molecule (e.g., Ab) can be helpful. It is important to identify NMs with optimum characteristics (e.g., morphology and surface functionality) to efficiently accommodate the highest number of Ab and therapeutic agent molecules. The long-term stability of most developed theranostic conjugates remains unknown. The stability and shelf life of NM-based theranostics should be tested, which will help to determine their practical applications. Additionally, any possibility of leakage or degradation of the imaging/contrast agent should also be tested.

Most studies of NM-based theranostics have been performed on small animals such as mice. Their successful translation to clinical trials and commercialization for human use is a challenging endeavor. The toxicity of NMs poses another major obstacle because it is difficult to distinguish from the toxicity of their macroscale counterparts (Cheng et al., 2020). Determining the time required for the total clearance of the theranostic agent from systemic circulation is another challenge that must be met before laboratory studies can become clinical trials. Even minute quantities of non-biodegradable and non-excreted NMs left in the human body could produce serious clinical consequences such as toxicity and fatality. Therefore, a critical evaluation of the long-term effects of these theranostics on the human body and the possibility of their accumulation in biological systems is also required. NMs with intrinsic emission properties (e.g., QDs, Cdots, UCNPs, GNPs, GQDs, and MOFs) can be used to immobilize therapeutic agents because of their pendant surface functionalities. Those NMs can also serve as multimodal imaging and therapeutic platforms because they have plenty of free binding sites for therapeutic agents. Likewise, the success of Abs-based cancer therapy critically depends upon our understanding about the interaction between these therapeutic drugs and their ability to: (a) recruit immunomodulatory molecules (which are otherwise inactivated by the malignant cells) and (b) inhibit angiogenesis and proliferation of tumor cells. Theoretically, the off-site toxicity of therapeutic Abs should be minimal owing to their selective affinity for surface antigens or epitopes. The morphological properties and well-defined metabolism of immunomodulatory Abs are also suggested to reduce their toxicity (Brennan et al., 2010). However, under clinical settings, many Abs have shown to produce off-site toxic effects. Primarily, the off-site toxic effects of therapeutic Abs (e.g., cetuximab, Erbitux, and trastuzumab) can be ascribable to their exaggerated pharmacological response (immunosuppression or immune activation) and/or cross-reactivity with differentially expressed antigens on non-target cell population. As the cancer cells seldomly express tumor-specific antigens, Abs against tumor associated antigens are suggested to reduce the specificity for normal cells with the minimum toxic effects. Some notable tumor associated antigens under clinical trials for antibody-drug conjugate targeting include CD138, 5T3, and mesothelin (Abdollahpour-Alitappeh et al., 2019).

Abs that inhibit immunoregulatory molecules are collectively referred to as the immune checkpoint inhibitors. Since the approval of anti-cytotoxic T-lymphocyte–associated antigen 4 (anti-CTLA-4) (ipilimumab) for treatment of melanoma and NSCLC by US-FDA in 2011, immune checkpoint inhibitors have become gold standard in cancer therapy (Desnoyer et al., 2020; Diesendruck and Benhar 2017). The clinical and pre-clinical applications of immune checkpoint inhibitors and programmed cell death (PD-1) mAbs (e.g., ipilimumab (a CTLA-4 inhibitor), nivolumab and pembrolizumab (PD-1 inhibitors)) have consistently been increasing. As a result, their adverse effects have also been reported in a number of clinical trials. The scarcity of data related to toxicity of such mAbs has given rise to data mining approaches such as signal detection algorithms. These algorithms are used to explore medical databases for analysing the toxicity data to assess the prospective associations between therapeutic Abs and their adverse effects (Harpaz et al., 2013). To this end, the potential toxic effects of immunomodulatory therapeutic mAbs have been documented by US-FDA in a database called Adverse Event Reporting System (FAERS) (Ji et al., 2019). Such database is expected to strengthen the US-FDAs’ strategies for post marketing monitoring of the approved drugs. A detailed report on the FAERS database and data collection approaches for the toxic effects of immunomodulatory therapeutic mAbs was provided by Ji et al. (2019). Overall, the intrinsic toxicity of NMs has necessitated the development of a discrete field of an emerging technology called ‘Nanotoxicology’.

For NMs that yield low emissions or have no natural emissions, dyes can be explored as candidate cancer imaging agents. Among them, NIR-emitting dyes are promising for tumor imaging because of their low background scattering and improved tissue penetration over dyes with emissions in the visible range (Kumar et al., 2019; Lee et al., 2017). However, most NIR-emitting structures have critical challenges, such as the formation of images with poor spatial resolution and low sensitivity, low quantum yield, limited hydrophilicity, and limited photostability. Those challenges could limit the theranostic applications of attaching NIR dyes to NMs.

It was suspected that conjugating a therapeutic or targeting agent with NMs could cause a decrease in the imaging signals (Azhdarzadeh et al., 2016; Jing et al., 2013; Mewada et al., 2014; Tian et al., 2014b). Though conjugated NMs could exhibit enhanced imaging signals upon release of the therapeutic agent in the body, that could lead to false positive signals and inaccurate interpretation of the imaging data. If the imaging agent accumulates in the diseased area, a slight increase or decrease in the signals is probably insignificant. Still, to obtain accurate signals, such discrepancies should be included in future research. Additionally, NM-based theranostics should be uniformly effective in the diverse physiological conditions of the body. Overall, high conjugation efficiency for the drug and imaging agent, high quantum efficiency, non-toxicity for non-specific sites, complete clearance from the body after application, and ensuring high sensitivity and high specificity should be the main research targets for future NM-based theranostics.

Another major challenge in design and development of NM-Ab conjugates for cancer theranostics is their instability in complex biological environment. Thus, it is critical to prepare NM-Ab conjugates with acceptable stability under biological environments. To address this problem, Raoof et al. (2012) proposed directional conjugation of glycosylated anti-EGFR (C225) Ab with GNPs using a heterofunctional (amine functional group at one end and thiol functionality at the other end) alkane linker. The prepared C225-GNP nanoconjugate showed their appreciable stability under physiological conditions with a negligible aggregation at pH = 7.4 for >3 days (Raoof et al., 2012). In another study, PET imaging of 64Cu labeled NOTA-GO-FSHR-mAb showed ~91% of signal retainment over 24 h incubation in cbgLuc-MDA-MB-231 (cbgLuc = click beetle green luciferase) tumor grafted mice (Yang et al., 2016). The slow decay rate in radioactive signal highlighted the excellent stability of the nanoconjugate under in vivo conditions. The stability of various NM-Ab conjugates in aqueous and physiological conditions has been investigated by many other researchers as well (Chen et al., 2016d; Gibson et al., 2018; Han et al., 2019; Hassan et al., 2016; Nasrollahi et al., 2019; Wu et al., 2019). The results of these studies confirmed that appreciable stability of NM-Ab conjugates can be achieved under both in vitro and in vivo conditions by fine tuning the physicochemical characteristics of the NMs. However, the high acidic environment of cancer cells warrants continuous probing of the synthesis and the conjugation protocols to achieve substantial stability of NM-Ab conjugates. Another demanding issue towards successful clinical translation of NM-Ab conjugates is lack of sufficient clinical evidence about the interactions occurring at nano-bio interface. In a majority of the studies conducted to date, it is difficult to achieve a satisfactory correlation between in vitro and in vivo experiments.

Finally, the transformation of NMs and their conjugates from laboratory synthesis to bulk scale production is a significant technical obstacle. A search made on Sigma-Aldrich website revealed that even small fractions of various NMs (e.g., GNPs (100 mL), magnetic NPs (5 mL), and CNTs (1 g)) cost in the range of 238–1190 USD (Table 4 ). Further, even minute quantities (0.5 mg) of some commercially available mAbs (e.g., Cetuximab, Bevacizumab, and Trastuzumab) cost as low as 275 USD (Table 4). The procedures for the formation of NM-conjugate further escalates the synthesis cost due to the involvement of multistage purification processes. Assuming the 100% yield of a NM-Ab conjugate reaction, the synthesis cost of a typical NM-Ab conjugate (1 g) will be > 1000 USD. Considering the potential clinical advantages associated with the multifunctional NM-Ab conjugates, it is essential to develop well-designed experimental protocols to reduce and justify their manufacturing costs.

In view of the challenges described above, researchers are trying to develop alternative probe molecules, which can provide highly specific recognition of cancer antigens alike to the cancer specific Abs. In this regard, functional nucleic acids such as aptamers, DNAzymes, and DNA-based nanomachines have emerged as suitable alternatives to Abs for cancer theranostic applications. Specifically, aptamers exhibit many unique features such as three-dimensional structural orientation, facile and economic synthesis in laboratory conditions, and highly specific nucleotide sequence (Peng et al., 2020). These characteristics enable the aptamers to recognize the target molecules with remarkable specificity, which is comparable to that of therapeutic Abs. Accordingly, many researchers have investigated cancer theranostic potential of aptamers (Jolly et al., 2015; Lei et al., 2018; Pang et al., 2018; Ye et al., 2016). Nonetheless, like Abs, aptamers do have certain limitations (off-site toxicity and stability) for the actual clinical translation from laboratory practices to real world situations. Extensive and parallel research efforts are required to develop Abs and other probe molecules (e.g., aptamers) as state-of-the-art technologies for better cancer management.

According to a WHO report from 2018, 9.6 million lives worldwide are lost to cancer every year. Approximately 70% of cancer-related deaths occur in countries suffering from poverty and low per-capita income. As discussed above, the utility of conventional clinical diagnosis methods is often restricted in the early period of cancer occurrence. The physiological and clinical complications associated with cancer occurrence and progression warrant the development of theranostic protocols to help increase the overall survival time of patients. Actively targeting cancer tissues is an important approach to developing effective theranostic methods.

In this regard, Abs are a valuable tool for developing theranostic materials for the timely detection and treatment of cancer. The considerable increase in the number of Ab-based cancer theranostics is attributable to their biological origin and excellent affinity for tumor markers. The uniqueness of Abs as biomolecules produces their cellular recognition and targeting abilities. The major signal transduction mechanisms of Ab-based theranostics are ADCC, ADCP, and CDC, each of which can form a basis for developing future Ab-based cancer theranostic regimens. The chemical modalities of Abs facilitate their immobilization on the surfaces of NMs such as GNPs, MNPs, QDs, carbon NMs, UCNPs, and MSNs. Ab–NM conjugation is achieved by various covalent and non-covalent methods using zero-length CLs, homobifunctional CLs, and heterobifunctional CLs.

The major design concept for using Ab–NM conjugates as theranostic platforms requires three integral components: (a) a drug carrier, (b) a theranostic payload, and (c) the Ab itself as a targeting moiety. All these constituents have been extensively investigated for the development of customized theranostic applications. Most inorganic drug carriers (e.g., GNPs, carbon-based NMs, and UCNPs) and hybrid nanocarriers (e.g., MOFs) offer dual functioning for imaging and cargo upload. Such dual modality carriers could substantially reduce the burden of separately uploading or encapsulating imaging modalities. Furthermore, the numerous pendant functional groups on various NMs provide directional conjugation for Ab molecules on their surfaces.

The second component, theranostic payloads, comprises therapeutic compounds (chemotherapeutic agents, hormonal therapeutic agents, and immunotherapeutic agents) and bioimaging payloads (e.g., QDs and UCNPs). NMs such as GNPs, QDs, MNPs, and UCNPs have been used as exciting and innovative tools for cancer theranostics. For instance, these NMs can be modified to exhibit stimulus-response (e.g., pH- and temperature-dependent) drug release properties for enhanced and sensitive tumor attenuation. As explained above, the hyperthermia offered by certain NMs (e.g., UCNPs and MNPs) in conjugation with Ab targeting substantially enhance their performance through PTT and PDT. Drug resistance mechanisms in tumor tissues can also be subdued by combining chemotherapeutics with Ab-targeted PTT and PDT. The development of such combinatorial theranostic protocols has generated unprecedented growth in the development of Ab–NM conjugates.

As understanding of the molecular biology of cancer has advanced, numerous mAb-based drugs for cancer treatment have been commercialized (e.g., nivolumab, ipilimumab, and imatinib) or are in clinical trials (e.g., trastuzumab, pembrolizumab, bevacizumab, nivolumab, and cetuximab). Moreover, combining Ab–NM conjugate-based theranostics with conventional chemotherapeutic drugs seems to be a promising option. However, even with the current state of the art, product-based technological innovations to enable clinical trials of Ab–NM conjugates are needed because most Ab–NM theranostic systems are still in the proof-of-concept stage of development. In addition, NM-Ab conjugates do suffer from certain limitations such as instability, off-site toxicity, and high synthesis cost of both Abs and NMs. These theranostic platforms must be tested in various animal models to ascertain their future, not only for clinical trials but also to improve understanding of the developing field of nanotoxicology. Further, a better correlation between in vitro and in vivo testing of NM-Ab conjugates is also required towards developing state of the art commercial technologies for cancer management. A deeper knowledge of the molecular events associated with cancer will facilitate the development of Ab–NM conjugates as gene delivery vehicles.
